doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
REGIONAL CONTEXT
Del City is within Oklahoma County and is a part of the Oklahoma City metropolitan area. Interstate
40 runs directly through Del City and connects to downtown Oklahoma City. On the southeastern
border of Del City lies Midwest City and the Tinker Air Force Base.
Del City is nestled in the Cross Timbers eco-region, a transitional area between the wetter and
forested areas of Eastern Oklahoma and the plains of Western Oklahoma.
The purpose of Map 1.1, Regional Context is to emphasize Del City’s prime location. Within a 2.5
to 5 mile radius there are many destinations which attract locals and tourists from all over the globe.
This map highlights just a few of the destinations close to Del City. 

KEY THEMES
During a series of community kick-off events, in November 2022, there was a dialogue about the core values
and themes of Del City which should guide the development of the comprehensive plan. These values reflect
the community of Del City as a whole and create the foundation for directing future planning efforts to
ensure that they align with the accepted values of the community. Out of the community feedback received
in Del City through conversations and direct comment through a survey, open houses, and stakeholder
meetings, some key themes and takeaways began to emerge. A summary of these themes and values can
be seen in the word cloud below. These essential themes led to the creation of the vision statement on the
next page.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
VISION
One of the most important components of a comprehensive plan is the community’s vision for the future.
This vision was derived through the public engagement process and captured in the vision statement, goals,
strategies and implementation actions.
The community’s vision for the future is also embodied in the City’s adopted Future Land Use Plan.
The Future Land Use Plan identifies differing areas of character which can guide future land use and
development decisions. As the Future Land Use Plan is implemented it creates the Del City of tomorrow as
envisioned by the community. In this regard, the vision identifies how the community believes Del City should
approach the future and what purpose this plan serves to help the community get there.
The vision sets the stage for a series of eight plan goals, which frame the future development and plan
recommendations set out in Chapter 3, Del City Reimagined and Chapter 4, Special Focus Areas.

PLAN GOALS
Public input should drive the plan direction and lead to discernible action items and outcomes. The input of
the Del City community was measured during the engagement process and ultimately led to the vision and
goals of this Plan. It is clear that the community is passionate about their city but agree that Del City is not
achieving it’s full potential.
The eight goals described on the next page form the basis for a series of commitments and initiatives in
Chapter 3, Del City Reimagined. Each of the eight goals are equally important to the plan and are not
ranked by importance. The plan goals and vision also inform the direction of Chapter 4, Special Focus
Areas, which establishes small area plans for the three focus areas within Del City. The Envision Del
City Comprehensive Plan is incremental and meant to be the first of many phases towards achieving the
community’s vision. These goals should serve as a starting point to lead Del City to its full potential.
From Goals to Action
Many of these goals are timeless statements of future values that will last beyond the plan horizon, yet
signal a clear mandate to quickly get to work pursuing the desired outcomes. These plan goals are the
guiding principles for decision-making priorities for the City over the next 10 to 20 years. The goals provide a
context and clear direction for government actions, partnerships, and leadership decisions, ensuring a longterm mindset based on the community’s values. In the subsequent chapters of this plan, the communitysupported vision and goals are put to work in the future development framework and agenda of strategies
and actions to position Del City for the future.

text:
POPULATION PROJECTIONS
The 1950 Census recorded a population of 2,504 within Del City. With the growth from Tinker Air
Force Base and the growth pressure from Oklahoma City, Del City grew over 500% from 1950 to 1980.
The current population in Del City is approximately 21,822 per Esri and the U.S. Census estimates.
Population projections are estimated to grow at 0.3% compounded annual growth rate with a total
population of 24,678 by 2045.

PLANNING AREA
The planning area consists of the city proper known as Del City. It is approximately seven and
a half square miles in area and is located six miles from Oklahoma City, Oklahoma. Del City is
bordered by Oklahoma City to the north, west, and south and Midwest City to the east. Del City has
approximately16 parks, ranging in size from pocket parks of 0.1 acres to 9 acres. The school district is
shared with Midwest City and their are eight total schools within the city limits. Highlighted on Map 2.1
Planning Area Context Map, are the three focus areas that will be expanded upon in further detail
in Chapter 4, Special Focus Areas. The city has started to see a trend with the population growing
by 2.3% since 2010. It is likely this growth trend will continue and emphasizes the importance of
planning for the future. Given the likely growth, the City is open to opportunities for expansion should
the opportunity arise.

COMMUNITY SERVICES AND
FACILITIES
COMMUNITY EVENTS
Del City hosts several special events throughout
the year that attract local and regional attendees.
These events are organized collaboratively with city
departments or other community organizations.
The events offer a multi-generational experience for
citizens and create a lively community. A few of the
annual events are as follows:
• Armed Forces Day
• Holiday Decorating Contest
• Trunk or Treat
• Small Business Markets
• Annual Chamber Auction
• Veterans Day Ceremony
DEL CITY LIBRARY
A new state-of-the-art public library was opened
in Del City in 2021. The library offers a variety of
services including access to computers, free WiFi,
copy/scan/faxing/printing services, a children’s
area, programs and events, study rooms and
meeting rooms.
PARKS & RECREATION
The Parks & Recreation Department provides more
than 200 acres of parkland and three miles of
bicycle and walking trails, the Eagle Harbor Aquatic
Center, Outdoor Fitness Court at Patriot Park,
and the Community Center. Along with those city
amenities, there are sixteen (16) parks throughout
the city. Some of the larger parks within Del City are
as follows:
• AWACS Park and Tornado Memorial
• Rotary Park and Community Garden
• Sequoyah Park
• Townsend Park
• High Spirits Park
• Cheyenne Park
• Eagle Lake
• Ray Trent Park & Wiggly Field
• Patriot Park & Women’s Veteran Memorial
• Cherokee Park
• Edwards Park
• Legion Park
• Oliver Park
• Alfred P. Murrah Park

text:
UTILITIES
The city relies on a diversified water supply system
to meet the needs of its residents and businesses.
The current water supply includes 12 wells that draw
groundwater and surface water is sourced from
Lake Thunderbird. The city’s water treatment plants
have a combined capacity of 5.2 million gallons per
day (MGD), while the average daily water usage
stands at 1.6 MGD. This indicates that there is
currently sufficient capacity to meet the city’s water
demands, which include serving 7,696 residential
customers, 564 commercial establishments, 20
industrial facilities, 7 institutional properties, and
8 municipal entities, encompassing the entire city.
However, it is essential to address upgrades to
water facilities to ensure the continued availability
of clean and safe water for the growing population
and to maintain the reliability of the supply.
In terms of wastewater management, Del City
operates a wastewater treatment plant with a
current capacity of 2.1 MGD, a portion of the
6.6 MGD plant’s total capacity. The wastewater
system effectively serves the entire city, with
7,000 residential connections, 550 commercial
establishments, 20 industrial facilities, 7
institutional properties, and 8 municipal entities
all connected to the sewer system. At present,
there are no immediate concerns regarding the
plant’s capacity to treat wastewater. However, lift
station upgrades are necessary to maintain the
efficiency and reliability of the sewer system. It is
also recommended that a comprehensive study
be conducted on the city’s sewer lines to address
inflow and infiltration issues.
The city should consider the development of a
Water and Wastewater Master Plan to ensure
long-term sustainability and resilience in the face
of potential future challenges and population
growth. These initiatives will play a crucial role
in safeguarding Del City’s water and wastewater
infrastructure and securing a dependable supply for
its residents and businesses.

text:
ECONOMIC MARKET ANALYSIS
OFFICE
The office market in Del City is strong with 383,000
square feet of building space (in 52 buildings) and a
low vacancy rate of 2.0%. Market rent is at $16.24
per square foot with a healthy vacancy rate of 2.0%.
Del City could expect to continue the last decade’s
annual trend of 8,000 square feet of office space
net absorption in the next 12 months. Absorption
refers to the change in occupancy over a given
time period. Net absorption is the measure of total
square feet occupied (tenant moving in or additional
space leased) less the space vacated.
RETAIL
Retail space in Del City is made up of 185 buildings,
with a combined total of 1.6 million square feet.
Market rent is $12.87 per square foot with a vacancy
rate of 4.6%. 12-month net absorption is at 44,600
square feet. More recently absorption has been
growing since 2020, more than doubling from 2020
to 2021.
INDUSTRIAL
The City of Del City currently has 1 million square
feet of industrial space across 80 existing buildings.
Market rent is currently high at $7.71 per square
foot, and the vacancy rate is 8.7%. It is expected
that Del City could absorb 18,800 square feet of
office space over the next 12 months.

HOUSING & NEIGHBORHOODS
HOUSING STOCK CHARACTERISTICS
There are a total of 9,028 households in Del City with the majority of homes (63.5%) having been built
between 1940 and 1969. This gives a majority of the homes and businesses in Del City a charming, midcentury modern aesthetic. Within the total number of housing units 52.9% are owner-occupied, 38.2%
are renter-occupied, and 8.8% are vacant. Vacancy is needed in a market to allow for transition and to
not encourage a housing shortage which can reduce affordability. The 8.8% vacancy rate in Del City is
considered healthy in comparison to the State of Oklahoma (12.3%) and Oklahoma County (10.1%).
Demographic projections by Esri for 2027 indicate that the tenancy in the Del City housing market is
expected to remain relatively stable with the share of owner-occupied housing units growing slightly from
52.9% in 2022 to 53.9% in 2027. The majority of homes in Del City are single family detached at 82.4%.
The second largest style of housing are apartments with three to nine units in structure at 9.1%. The value
of single family detached, owner-occupied homes is currently between $50,000 and $99,999 (34.7%). This
same category of housing units is expected to be valued between $100,000 and $199,999 by 2027 (33.1%).
Currently, over half of the housing units within Del City are valued under $200,000.
SINGLE-FAMILY HOUSING
Single-family building permits saw a rise in 2016 and
2017 with a total of 48 building permits approved.
In recent years single-family building permits have
tapered off, down to three in 2020 and rose slightly
again in 2021 to seven. In an effort to understand
the capacity of the single-family residential market,
an analysis was performed using historical data and
projected growth rates. It is estimated that Del City
could capture up to 25 new owner-occupied, singlefamily detached units annually. Roughly 32% of the
demand is for units at a price point below $200,000,
a total of eight units annually. There is also demand
for an additional five owner-occupied units annually
in the form of duplex/triplex/fourplex, townhomes,
rowhomes, or condos. This represents just over
16% of the owner-occupied demand within Del
City. These annual demand numbers represent what
the City of Del City is anticipated to absorb across
all housing types based upon current income and
projected growth rates.
MULTIFAMILY
There are currently ten existing multifamily
complexes in Del City, totaling 1,135 units. The
current market rent is $664 per unit ($0.87 per
square feet) with a vacancy rate of 5.8%. In addition
to single-family demand, an analysis on the region
was also performed to understand the renteroccupied residential market. It is estimated Del
City could capture and absorb 21 units annually of
renter-occupied housing in the form of multifamily
residential. There is additional capacity for 6 units
of renter-occupied housing in other formats such
as duplex/triplex/fourplex or single-family build
to rent. As stated previously, these annual demand
numbers do not represent what Del City must
absorb but represent the annual amount Del City
could absorb if development of all types and price
points were targeted.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
Zoning is a tool cities use to ensure compatibility between land uses, maintain a balance between residence
and commerce, and allow for general certainty as to how land will develop in the future. Del City’s zoning
regulations determine the mix of land uses within the city limits, currently consisting primarily of residential,
commercial, and industrial uses. Commercial zoning is located mainly on Interstate 40 and other arterials
such as Sooner Road, Reno Avenue, 15th Street, and 29th Street. Residential zoning is spread all over the
city as displayed on Map 2.2, Existing Zoning Map. Shown in Table 2.1, Existing Zoning are the zoning
districts and their existing acreage.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
The relatively flat topography of Oklahoma can lead to rapid drainage and flash flooding during storms. Del
City has five waterways within the city limits which contribute to flooding. These include Crutcho Creek,
Cherry Creek, Branch Creek and Crooked Oak Creek and their tributaries, as well as the North Canadian
River. Approximately 29% of land or 1,400 acres in Del City are located within the floodplain.
Del City is situated at the bottom of two major drainage basins. A large amount of stormwater conveyed
through the City originates in areas outside of the City’s control. Flooding in the City is mainly caused by the
Crutcho and Cherry Creeks. See Map 2.3, Floodplain for locations of floodplains within the city.
The City of Del City Community Development Department is committed to protecting the health and safety
of Del City residents. With further growth usually comes expanded impervious cover and the possibility for
an increase in flooding. As a result, it is important to ensure working drainage structures and/or channels
are available to manage flood conditions when necessary.

section: COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT OVERVIEW & PUBLIC OPEN HOUSE & ADVISORY COMMITTEE
text:
COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT OVERVIEW
To ensure this plan embodies the vision of the community, an extensive public engagement process was
completed to identify the needs and desires of the community. The information collected during this process
created the foundation for this plan.

STAKEHOLDER MEETINGS
The project team and city staff hosted two stakeholder focus groups for economic development and city
improvement in Del City to gauge interests and concerns of community leaders and stakeholders. Both
stakeholder meetings were held November 3, 2022, as informal, round table-style discussions. Information
collected during these meetings helped shape the community survey and open house questions and
generated ideas that helped to form the key themes and takeaways for this plan and could be incorporated
later as recommendations.
In addition to the two general stakeholder meetings, three focus area workshops were held to discuss
three focus areas within Del City. These meetings were held on February 9, 2023 to discuss the future of
the selected focus areas. Findings from these meeting combined with input from the Advisory Committee
formed the basis of the recommendations for the focus areas in Chapter 4, Special Focus Areas.

PUBLIC OPEN HOUSE
The first public engagement event was hosted at
the Del City Community Center. It was conducted
in an open house format with seven informational
boards describing the comprehensive planning
process, and how it applies to Del City. This event
also contained seven interactive engagement
boards to gauge the community’s interest in various
topics and their vision for the future of Del City.
Advertising materials for this event were created
by the project team and distributed by city staff
to encourage attendance and participation in the
comprehensive planning process.

ADVISORY COMMITTEE
The Advisory Committee, comprised of City
Council and Planning Commission, met at
key intervals throughout the process to
provide invaluable input and guidance for the
Comprehensive Plan.
Through a series of meetings, the Advisory
Committee guided the development of the
plan by identifying and evaluating issues and
needs, assisting with setting priorities, and
providing comments on the plan’s preliminary
and final recommendations.

## Online Survey Summary

An online survey was open to the public to gather community input for the development of the Comprehensive Plan. The survey was available from October 14, 2022, through December 7, 2022. The survey was offered online and the opportunity was given to complete the survey at a community event. The focus of the survey was to gain an understanding of the desires of residents for the future of Del City. The 19-question survey included a variety of topics such as demographics, overall quality of life, satisfaction with city services, transportation, housing and neighborhoods, economic development, community development, and priority issues. The public input received during this effort was an important source of information for developing the community vision and key themes. A full copy of the survey is provided in Appendix A.

---

## Top Attractions to Promote the Community

1. Retail & Shopping  
2. Festivals & Events  
3. Culinary Attractions  

---

## Top Areas of Satisfaction in Del City’s Performance

| Ranking | Areas of Importance |
|--------|--------------------|
| 1      | As a place to work |
| 2      | As a place to live |
| 3      | As a place to do business |
| 4      | Overall quality of city services (i.e., water, sewer, electricity, road maintenance) |
| 5      | As a place to retire |

---

## Important Factors in Economic Development

| Ranking | Areas of Importance |
|--------|--------------------|
| 1      | Branding & image of Del City |
| 2      | Amenities/attractions in Del City |
| 3      | Aesthetics/appearance of development |
| 4      | Internet access |
| 5      | Available jobs |

---

## Top Priorities for the Future of Del City

- Schools & Education  
- Public Services & Utilities  
- Neighborhoods  

---

## What Do You Love About Del City?

Common themes expressed by respondents include:

- Community  
- Location and proximity  
- People and friendliness  
- Employment opportunities  
- History and hometown identity  
- Affordability  
- Atmosphere  
- Leadership  
- Activities and amenities

text:
FRAMEWORK FOR THE FUTURE
It is clear from interactions with the community that there is both optimism and energy about Del City’s
future. The community’s vision for tomorrow calls for continued investment towards efforts that bring
economic prosperity, exciting family-friendly amenities, and safe, attractive neighborhoods. It also calls for
a unified framework, one that leads toward an enduring place to call home. These ideals have formed the
foundation of the plan recommendations found in this chapter.
Public input should drive the plan direction and lead to discernible action items and outcomes. The input of
the Del City community was measured during the engagement process and ultimately led to the vision and
goals of this Plan. These goals are timeless statements of future values that the City will continually strive
for. Under each goal statement is a series of strategies for how to make progress towards each goal. And
supporting each strategy are actions for the city staff, elected and appointed leaders, and the community to
take to progress towards the goal.
The rest of this chapter identifies a series of strategies and actions to achieve these goals over time. It
is understood that not every commitment or initiative will be realized in the near-term or, in some cases,
during the time horizon of this plan. As such, Chapter 5, Implementation Plan, sets out a prioritized
implementation action plan which provides greater clarity as to the community’s short- and long-term plan
of action for moving forward.

section: GOAL 1: REVITALIZATION: FUTURE LAND USE MAP
text:
GOAL 1: REVITALIZATION
SUPPORT QUALITY REVITALIZATION IN DEL CITY
FUTURE LAND USE MAP
Strategy 1.1 pertains to the adoption and
implementation of a Future Land Use Plan. The
next several pages, from page 32 to page 51,
outline the Future Land Use Map and descriptions
of the land use categories. Strategy 1.2 pertains to
updates to development codes which would assist
the community’s ability to achieve higher quality
development in Del City.
The Future Land Use Map is an important
component of the community build-out vision,
focusing on how land uses are distributed
across the planning area. The Future Land Use
Map graphically depicts the preferred building
patterns in the city limits and is a strategic tool
for consideration of development and capital
infrastructure. Building consensus in a plan requires
acknowledging today's built environment while also
recognizing that the future will harbor changes to
how we live and function. The process of creating
this plan was careful to balance the desired growth
vision, market realities, public infrastructure and
services, and consideration of what is on the ground
today.
This planning framework is built upon a characterbased philosophy that focuses equally on building
form and site characteristics as it does land use and
is contextually-sensitive to the desired development
areas. The plan reflects a desire for more
commercial and/or retail land use, a wider variety
of housing options, pedestrian connected green
spaces, and overall creation of sense of place. The
total amount of projected acres and percentage
breakdowns are shown in Land Use Breakdown to
the right.
The map of future land uses is depicted on the
next page, page 33, and descriptions of usage and
interpretation begin on page 36.

section: STRATEGY 1.1: ENSURE CONSISTENT APPLICATION OF THE FUTURE LAND USE MAP TO GUIDE THE FUTURE OF DEL CITY.
text:
STRATEGY 1.1: ENSURE CONSISTENT APPLICATION OF THE FUTURE
LAND USE MAP TO GUIDE THE FUTURE OF DEL CITY.
ACTION 1.1.1
CONSISTENTLY UTILIZE THE FUTURE LAND
USE MAP WHEN REVIEWING DEVELOPMENT
PROPOSALS AND REZONING APPLICATIONS
TO ENSURE FUTURE DEVELOPMENT IS
COMPATIBLE WITH THE CITY’S VISION. USE
THE FUTURE LAND USE MAP TO ASSIST
LANDOWNERS, PROSPECTIVE BUILDERS,
AND OTHER ENTITIES TO PLAN FOR THE
FUTURE.
The Future Land Use Map serves as a proactive
planning instrument, allowing stakeholders to
anticipate future development trends and patterns
and creates adjacency predictability to encourage
private investment. It assists in identifying areas
suitable for residential, commercial, industrial,
or recreational purposes, among others. By
referring to the Future Land Use Map, landowners,
prospective builders, and other entities gain a clear
understanding of the intended land uses and zoning
regulations for specific areas. The plan should also
be used to identify and inform recommendations for
any future small area plans, development master
plans, and budget improvements.
ACTION 1.1.2
CREATE AND FOLLOW A PROCESS FOR
FUTURE LAND USE MAP AMENDMENTS
TO ENSURE THAT PROPOSED CHANGES
ARE ANALYZED CONSISTENTLY WITH THE
COMPREHENSIVE PLAN VISION.
The Future Land Use Map plays a vital role in
promoting consistency and fairness in the decisionmaking process. By basing evaluations and
recommendations on the Future Land Use Map,
authorities can provide transparent and objective
assessments of development proposals. Cities
change over time, when changes are needed to
be made to the Future Land Use Map, ensure the
changes are well justified and documented.

text:
The following pages include defining attributes and decision-making guidance for each of the land use
categories. The information presented forecasts the intent of each of these future places and is intended as
a helpful guide when considering future development.
The descriptions and representative imagery can help decision making regarding land development
appropriateness, character, and compatibility. The goal is to ensure that a proposed development pattern
will complement or align with existing land uses, proximate land uses, and general character of an area
(i.e., building design, site layout, scale). The identified uses were prepared with the intent of providing
balance between various uses and specific information to aid in their application as the City responds to
development requests.
For most land use categories, a mix of uses is desired to provide balance and supportive synergy. These
percentages are included as an informative tool to describe the character and intent of the place type
and they should not be viewed as a strict regulation. The percentages of residential and commercial land
use should be used to determine appropriateness of a zoning proposal and evaluate the mix of uses. The
percentage breakdown should be applied across larger geographic areas rather than by parcel, common
ownership, or proposed zoning area. While an individual development proposal could include a single land
use and the overall place type mix would still be maintained, it is best practice to encourage some amount of
the targeted mix. A single land use may also be appropriate when a parcel is too small to accommodate a mix
of uses.
Each land use category page contains the following components:

## Table 3.2 Land Use Components

| Attributes                     | Definitions                                                                 |
|--------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Intent and Character           | A general description of the desired intent and character of development.   |
| Land Use Mix                   | Recommended desired mix of land uses.                                       |
| Appropriate Land Use Types     | Guidance for the primary, and in many cases, secondary uses that may be appropriate in certain areas. |
| Guidance and Interpretation    | Recommendations for decision-making in regards to zoning proposals, location and site considerations, and interpretation. |
| Representative Imagery         | Photo or graphic examples compatible with each land use category. These are intended to help provide a visual guide to the types of uses, structures, and scale that would be appropriate. |

text:
INTENT AND CHARACTER
The Neighborhood Residential category is intended
to be primarily single-family neighborhoods but
with a large variety of lot sizes, housing types, and
associated amenities such as parks, trails, and
schools. Conventional detached residential products
are typical while other types of residences such as
attached residential products can be considered
on a case by case basis. Accessory Dwelling
Units should be allowed on a case by case basis
but limited to be allowed as short-term rentals
for approximately1-3 years after construction.
New residential developments should prioritize
walkability.
Architectural styles, building height and massing
should be not vary dramatically unless thoughtfully
arranged. Streets are typically local and collector
sections with curb and gutter, sidewalks, consistent
speeds, and accessible driveways. Parks and
schools in the neighborhood should be well
integrated and accessible. In some occasions where
the neighborhood abuts a more significant street, a
personal service business, such as a day care, may
be appropriate if designed at a compatible scale.
Below are the recommended mixes of commercial
and residential land uses.

APPROPRIATE LAND USE TYPES
Commercial
At significant intersections, or in Planned Unit
Developments, neighborhood-serving retail,
personal service, and office uses are encouraged
with specific design standards and compatibility
criteria. These uses should typically be found along
higher-classification streets and sited at or near
intersections.
Residential
Single-family detached residential; Single-family
attached residential; Cottages; Townhouses.
Industrial
Not appropriate.
Institutional
Education (e.g., elementary schools) integrated into
neighborhoods as much as possible; Middle schools
and High Schools; Public assembly; and utility
and service uses (e.g., electrical substations, fire
stations, etc.).
Green Spaces
Parks and recreation facilities; natural areas; open
spaces; trail corridors; water quality features.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
INTENT AND CHARACTER
The Community Commercial category is intended for
mostly non-residential businesses of a nature, scale,
and design compatible with nearby residences.
Site design should be neighborhood-focused and
prioritize walkability, with compatible screening,
light shielding, attractive street landscaping, etc.
Properties in these areas may see significant peak
vehicle traffic, elevating access to a critical site
consideration. Buildings and setback areas should
have a thoughtful relationship to adjacent streets
and be pedestrian-friendly, with parking and
driveways diminished from the street view.
Upper-story residential or occasional highdensity residential can be supportable in some
circumstances. Density helps creates demand for
nearby business. Below are the recommended
mixes of commercial and residential land uses.

APPROPRIATE LAND USE TYPES
Commercial
Neighborhood-serving retail, personal service,
small-scale office buildings, day care, boutique
shops, and restaurant uses. Near major
intersections, supporting commercial to more
regional businesses such as hotels, grocery stores,
etc. may be appropriate.
Residential
Secondary uses within Community Commercial
should be medium density attached residential
(townhouses), upper-story residential lofts with
living units at no more than three stories, and
apartments where appropriate.
Industrial
Not appropriate.
Institutional
Places of public assembly (e.g., churches); and
utility and service uses (e.g., electrical substations,
fire stations, etc.).
Green Spaces
Places of public assembly (e.g., churches); and
utility and service uses (e.g., electrical substations,
fire stations, etc.).

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
INTENT AND CHARACTER
Interstate 40 is one of the major Oklahoma
highways and a unique Del City land use category.
Future development near this corridor is best
focused on retail, overnight accommodations,
employment, and destination services. Businesses
in this area serve Del City residents but are less
likely to be locally-owned. These businesses are the
face that many people see when driving through Del
City so it is important to give people reasons to exit
the highway.
These areas are intended to be developed with an
auto-oriented character, which means vehicles and
parking areas are a primary visual characteristic
from the street. Buildings may be large and bulky
with large parking areas geared towards vehicles.
Pedestrian safety is important but this land use area
will generally not be a location for high-priority trails
and sidewalks.
While many uses will front on service roads,
connectivity to the Del City street grid through
drives and backage roads is important. Access
management is recommended to maintain safe
traffic movement along these streets.
Future residential uses are discouraged. Below
are the recommended mixes of commercial and
residential land uses.

APPROPRIATE LAND USE TYPES
Commercial
Commercial retail, office, personal service uses,
and restaurants, including drive-throughs. These
can be individual parcels if pre-existing or as part
of a larger retail center. Hotels, grocery stores, and
similar regional uses are encouraged.
Residential
Future residential uses in a regionally-serving, autooriented corridor are not desirable. Should only be
considered for a mixed-use, walkable site where
residential is not adjacent to service roads.
Industrial
Light industrial and manufacturing can be
appropriate but not fronting service roads and
heavily buffered from nearby residential areas.
Institutional
Places of public assembly); and utility and service
uses (e.g., electrical substations, fire stations, etc.).
Educational uses are undesirable here.
Green Spaces
While public recreational uses (e.g., parks and
playgrounds) are not appropriate, trails and trail
connectivity are appropriate with protections.
Protection of natural spaces is critical and private
outdoor spaces are important for employees.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
INTENT AND CHARACTER
The Mixed-Use Community Category is envisioned
to provide a greater level of residential choice in a
variety of forms. This category best accommodates
residences built to meet the “missing middle”
segment of the housing marketplace. This may
include townhomes, attached dwellings, patio
homes, small-scale apartments in the appropriate
context, or a mix of several of these in a planned
setting. These highly activated areas include
integrated residential, retail, and small-scale
employment use.
While the primary intent of this category is to give
the city a greater variety of housing, the secondary
purpose it to increase the city’s sales tax base.
Mixed-Use land uses provide opportunities for
placemaking and provide more benefits to the city
as opposed to stand alone commercial or residential
uses.
Land uses are less distinguishable and less
regulated in this more urban environment, where
the focus is more on form than function. Important
characteristics include building relationship with
the street, pedestrian movement, signage, street
design, and public gathering places. Below are the
recommended mixes of commercial and residential
land uses.

APPROPRIATE LAND USE TYPES
Commercial
Contextual to location but intended to include
neighborhood and regional-serving retail, personal
service, restaurant (no drive-thru), and small-scale
office use.
Residential
Missing Middle housing types such as townhouses,
single-family detached, single-family attached,
cottages, urban lofts, and apartments in walkable
environments near commercial and open space.
Stand-alone apartments with no other land uses are
not desirable in this category.
Industrial
Not appropriate.
Institutional
Education (e.g., elementary and secondary
schools); institutional uses (e.g., places of public
assembly); utility and service uses (e.g., electrical
substations, fire stations, etc.).
Green Spaces
Parks and recreation facilities; natural areas; open
spaces; trail corridors; water quality features.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
INTENT AND CHARACTER
The Parks & Open Space category contains active
and passive parkland, trails, and open space.
These are largely comprised of lands which are
generally unsuitable for development including
lands within the floodway and floodplain. These
areas can be used for greens spaces either
programmed or not, trail development, and flood
mitigation. Existing trails should be connected into
a trail network. The trail network will be a regional
destination for people looking for trails, cycling,
and active transportation routes to employment
centers. From a community perspective, trails
are also opportunities for placemaking, including
art, historical elements, civic spaces, and wildlife
viewing. Trail connections should be made
frequently to adjacent developments to provide
access. Below are the recommended mixes of
commercial and residential land uses.

APPROPRIATE LAND USE TYPES
Commercial
Not appropriate.
Residential
Not appropriate.
Industrial
Not appropriate.
Institutional
Parkland, typically public, can include land privately
owned and maintained but has a public access
easement. Or, the public parkland may be a narrow
piece of land adjacent to private open space/
amenity area that adds to the feel of a larger green
space.
Other
Green space might include stormwater facilities
from adjacent development. These facilities need
to be designed thoughtfully, so as to create an
enhanced amenity within the green space and not
disrupt the stormwater purposes of managing
flooding. Stormwater facilities along these corridors
should be designed with a natural appearance and
not prevent publicly access.

text:
INTENT AND CHARACTER
Industrial includes uses that have potential
environmental impacts to air quality, water quality,
noise, etc. such as manufacturing, oil and gas
production, concrete or asphalt production,
and similar uses. This district should be properly
buffered from residential and other non-compatible
land uses.
Del City’s primary industrial area is in the north part
of town near the railroads and the river. This area
includes some legacy industrial users, along with
opportunity sites for new industrial development.
The area has good access to streets and railroads,
plus proximity to supporting services.
Buildings and lots are generally large due to the
space needed for the associated uses. Below
are the recommended mixes of commercial and
residential land uses. New industrial development
should provide setbacks and buffers to protect
nearby residential and retail. Below are the
recommended mixes of commercial and residential
land uses.

APPROPRIATE LAND USE TYPES
Commercial
Not appropriate.
Residential
Not appropriate.
Industrial
Heavy Industrial uses allowed on a limited or
conditional basis, depending upon the protections
required to decrease the amount of sound, dust,
vibration, odor, and glare, etc., which can occur offparcel or between future development categories.
Institutional
Utility and service uses (e.g., electrical substations,
fire stations, etc.).
Green Spaces
While public recreational uses (e.g., parks and
playgrounds) are not appropriate, trails and trail
connectivity are appropriate with protections.
Protection of natural spaces is critical and private
outdoor spaces are important for employees.
Other
Other uses can be permitted as limited or
conditional, if consistent with the character and
street relationship.

text:
INTENT AND CHARACTER
There are three special focus areas in Del City
generally located on either Reno Avenue or 29th
Street. Redevelopment or development for these
areas are further described in Chapter 4: Special
Focus Areas. Each area is intended to have a unique
character and serve as a catalyst for high-quality
redevelopment within Del City. As such no one
land use category fit for these special focus areas.
Each area will like be a combination of Community
Commercial, Mixed-Use Community, Neighborhood
Residential, and Parks & Open Space.
The Special Focus Area on Reno Avenue is intended
to reflect the communities vision for a more
family-friendly, walkable, destination centered
development near Eagle Lake Park. Further details
are discussed in Chapter 4: Special Focus Areas.
The Special Focus Area at 29th Street and Epperly
is intended to reflect the communities vision for
a traditional, main street type of development in
what is considered Olde Town. Further details are
discussed in Chapter 4: Special Focus Areas.
The Special Focus Area on 29th Street near the
Sunnylane Cemetery is intended to reflect the
communities desire for a new planned development
focused on diverse housing options and
commercial/retail development. Further details are
discussed in Chapter 4: Special Focus Areas.

APPROPRIATE LAND USE TYPES
Commercial
Context Specific. Where appropriate small-scale,
neighborhood-serving retail, personal service,
small-scale office buildings, day care, boutique
shops, and restaurant uses could be permitted.
Residential
Context Specific. When appropriate ensure a mix
of housing types are provided to diversify Del City’s
housing stock.
Industrial
Not appropriate.
Institutional
Places of public assembly (e.g., churches); and
utility and service uses (e.g., electrical substations,
fire stations, etc.).
Green Spaces
Context Specific. Parks and recreation facilities
should be required as a part of any new
development; natural areas; open spaces; trail
corridors; water quality features.
Other
Other uses can be permitted as limited or
conditional, if consistent with the character and
street relationship.

section: STRATEGY 1.2: UPDATE DEVELOPMENT CODES AND REGULATIONS TO ACHIEVE THE COMMUNITY’S DESIRED CHARACTER AND QUALITY.
text:
STRATEGY 1.2: UPDATE DEVELOPMENT CODES AND REGULATIONS TO
ACHIEVE THE COMMUNITY’S DESIRED CHARACTER AND QUALITY.

ACTION 1.2.1
UPDATE THE CITY’S DEVELOPMENT CODES
AND REGULATIONS TO INCORPORATE THE
QUALITY AND CHARACTER INTENT OF
EACH FUTURE LAND USE DESIGNATION.
Updating the city’s development codes and
regulations to incorporate the quality and character
intent of each future land use designation is a
vital step towards aligning development with the
long-term vision of Del City. By aligning zoning
with the character descriptions and future land
use categories, the City can revise its development
codes and regulations to accurately reflect the
envisioned development patterns. For example,
if the future land use plan designates an area for
mixed-use development with a vibrant pedestrianoriented character, the development codes and
regulations can be modified to require a certain
percentage of residential, commercial, and/or
recreational activities.
This might involve adjusting urban design guidelines
such as setback requirements, allowing for higher
building heights, promoting active transportation
options, and encouraging design elements that
enhance walkability and connectivity. By aligning
the development codes and regulations with
the character descriptions outlined in the future
land use plan, the City can promote consistency,
predictability, and the realization of a vibrant and
cohesive built environment. Based on the proposed
quality and character of each future land use
designation, incorporate these descriptions into the
existing zoning regulations. By incorporating design
standards into the development codes, Del City can
ensure that buildings and infrastructure are visually
appealing, well-integrated with the surrounding
environment, and conducive to a high quality of life
for residents.

ACTION 1.2.2
PROMOTE AND INCENTIVIZE GREEN
BUILDING PRACTICES AND LOW
IMPACT DEVELOPMENT FOR IMPROVED
INFRASTRUCTURE AND UTILITY
EFFICIENCIES.
By encouraging sustainable building techniques
and practices, the city can achieve environmental
benefits, reduce strain on infrastructure systems,
and enhance overall resource efficiency. One
effective strategy to promote green building
practices is by offering incentives to developers,
builders, and property owners who incorporate
sustainable features into their projects.

text:
GOAL 2: COMMERCIAL DEVELOPMENT
FACILITATE HIGH-QUALITY COMMERCIAL DEVELOPMENT

STRATEGY 2.1: DEVELOP AN EFFECTIVE AND AWARD WINING
ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT OPERATION TO FOSTER DEVELOPMENT
AND TALENT.

ACTION 2.1.1
DEVELOP A “BUY LOCAL” CAMPAIGN TO
CONTINUOUSLY PROMOTE LOCAL DEL CITY
BUSINESSES.
Sales tax is a critical component to Oklahoma city
tax revenue. Retail leakage creates lost revenue
and impacts local businesses. Promoting local
businesses through promotional campaigns,
programming, and events can mitigate sales tax
leakage and increase the vitality and sustainability
of local businesses. Increased revenue can
contribute additional spend within the community,
create capacity for greater local investment and
salaries.

ACTION 2.1.2
HOLD ANNUAL JOINT MEETINGS
BETWEEN THE ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
DEPARTMENT AND CHAMBER OF
COMMERCE TO COORDINATE AND AVOID
DUPLICATING EFFORTS.
Economic development is a multi-prong effort
that involves multiple departments and a wide
range of participants. The Economic Development
Department should foster regular conversations
with key stakeholders, including businesses,
property owners, city staff, city leaders, and the
Chamber of Commerce to explore common issues
and discuss how each member can contribute to the
overall economic health within Del City.

ACTION 2.1.3
JOIN OR MAINTAIN MEMBERSHIPS IN
REGIONAL BUSINESS ATTRACTION GROUPS
SUCH AS THE GREATER OKLAHOMA
CITY CHAMBER AND STATE CHAMBER OF
OKLAHOMA.
The Economic Development Department should also
participate in regional programs to collaborate with
other partners that promote economic development
including the Oklahoma Municipal League,
Greater Oklahoma City Economic Development,
International Council of Shopping Centers, and
Select Oklahoma. Del City should engage in regional
meetings, annual conferences, and volunteer
opportunities to maintain a “seat at the table” and
stay up to date in regional activity and keep Del City
top of mind for brokers, developers, site selectors,
tenants, and investors.

ACTION 2.1.4
CONSIDER ESTABLISHING A REVOLVING
LOAN FUND TO SUPPORT EXISTING
BUSINESS FAÇADE AND/OR AESTHETIC
IMPROVEMENTS.
Investment and reinvestment requires capital
and commitment. Explore partnerships with local
institutions, special program (similar to MAPS), or
the general fund to create a funding mechanism to
support redevelopment, revitalization, or incentives
for business programs.
The City should develop a clear written policy to
create performance-based policies that repay or
generate net-new revenue to enable long-term
funding of the program. The City should create
clear policies on the program to ensure incentives
are aligned with intended outcomes and that
participants are qualified and that the project would
not occur “but for” the incentive/participation. A
clear policy will enable management and increase
effectiveness.

## Goal 3: Placemaking

**Create unique areas with enhanced aesthetics and community activities.**

The following four pages are a high-level summary of what can be found in Chapter 4, Special Focus Areas. More details for these strategies and actions, including relevant context, images, and narrative explanations can be found in Chapter 4, Special Focus Areas.

---

## Strategy 3.1: Transform Eagle Lake into a Destination

- **Action 3.1.1**  
  Connect to the regional trail system.  

- **Action 3.1.2**  
  Construct an attractive and visible entrance at Reno Avenue.  

- **Action 3.1.3**  
  Add parking and food truck park infrastructure.  

- **Action 3.1.4**  
  Install new amenities and landscape features to support activation of the park.  

---

## Strategy 3.2: Enhance the Reno Avenue Streetscape to Welcome and Connect

- **Action 3.2.1**  
  Reconfigure Reno Avenue to support safe travel, connectivity, and aesthetic appeal.  

---

## Strategy 3.3: Support Strong Neighborhood Identity and Strengthen Commercial Properties

- **Action 3.3.1**  
  Support aesthetic improvements to properties with Reno Avenue frontage.  

- **Action 3.3.2**  
  Enhance neighborhood branding.  

- **Action 3.3.3**  
  Support evolution of land uses with new housing, small-scale industry, and renovated commercial buildings.  

---

## Strategy 3.4: Invest in a Main Street Destination at Olde Towne

- **Action 3.4.1**  
  Develop a placemaking strategy with further research and community engagement.  

- **Action 3.4.2**  
  Emphasize Epperly Drive as a classic, walkable Main Street environment.  

- **Action 3.4.3**  
  Consider a festival streetscape concept as an option for Epperly Drive.  

- **Action 3.4.4**  
  Refresh Rose Lane as an organized and attractive frontage along 29th Street.  

- **Action 3.4.5**  
  Explore a multi-purpose “town square” public space at the triangle near the fire department as a place for community programs.  

- **Action 3.4.6**  
  Pursue a district management approach.  

---

## Strategy 3.5: Leverage Vacant Property to Increase Del City’s Diversity of Housing and Neighborhoods

- **Action 3.5.1**  
  Promote a mix of density, uses, and housing styles on the large site.  

- **Action 3.5.2**  
  Address stormwater issue on the site with planned greens.  

---

## Strategy 3.6: Envision 15th Street as the Heart of a Visionary City Park System

- **Action 3.6.1**  
  Implement a wayfinding and branding strategy.  

- **Action 3.6.2**  
  Expand facilities for modern recreation preferences.  

- **Action 3.6.3**  
  Connect to the Oklahoma Regional Trail Network by adding connections between the Oklahoma River, Eagle Lake, Ray Trent Park, Tinker AFB, and Lake Stanley Draper.

text:
## Goal 4: Connect

**Enhance connections in Del City to provide more aesthetically pleasing, multi-functional corridors.**

---

## Thoroughfare Plan

The thoroughfare plan outlines the transportation network and infrastructure for Del City. It includes strategies for streets, pedestrian infrastructure, and other transportation-related elements. This thoroughfare plan will help identify the streets, highways, and major transportation corridors within Del City.

---

## Table 3.3 Street Classification

### Principal Arterial
1. Highways with a substantial volume of both intrastate and interstate travel.  
2. Highways catering to urbanized areas and the majority of small urban regions. The rural principal arterial system may also extend to an urban area if it either enters the urban boundary or is within 10 miles of it.  
3. Establish a cohesive network without dead-end connections, unless specific geographic or traffic flow conditions necessitate otherwise.  

---

### Minor Arterial
1. Establish connections between cities, larger towns, and other traffic generators, such as major resort areas, capable of attracting travelers from considerable distances. This interconnected network will facilitate both interstate and intercounty travel. The minor arterial system should extend its service to urban areas if it either enters the urban boundary or is within a 2-mile radius of it.  
2. Ensure appropriate spacing of arterial highways based on population density to ensure that all developed areas of the State have reasonable access to these streets.  
3. Direct focus on roads with longer trip lengths and higher travel density compared to those primarily served by rural collectors or local systems. Minor arterial routes should maintain relatively higher overall travel speeds and minimal disruption to through traffic.  

---

### Major Collector
1. Extend service to county seats that are not directly accessible via arterial routes, as well as to larger towns that direct connections to higher-level transportation systems. Additionally, cater to other intracounty traffic generators of similar importance, including consolidated schools, shipping points, county parks, significant mining and agricultural areas, etc.  
2. Establish links between these locations and nearby larger towns or cities, or connect them to routes with higher classification for improved accessibility and connectivity.  
3. Prioritize the provision of transportation services for more significant intracounty travel needs.  

---

### Minor Collector
1. Ensure appropriate spacing between collector streets, taking into account the population density, to effectively gather traffic from local streets and ensure that all developed areas are reasonably close to a collector street.  
2. Extend transportation service to the remaining smaller communities through the collector street system.  

---

### Local Streets
1. The main purpose of these streets is to grant access to adjacent land.  
2. They primarily cater to travel over shorter distances when compared to collector streets or other higher-level systems. It’s important to note that local streets encompass the rural mileage not classified under the principal arterial, minor arterial, or collector systems.

section: STRATEGY 4.1: MOVE PEOPLE THROUGH DEL CITY WITH A VARIETY OF MODAL TYPES AS PART OF A COMPREHENSIVE TRANSPORTATION NETWORK.
text:
STRATEGY 4.1: MOVE PEOPLE THROUGH DEL CITY WITH A VARIETY
OF MODAL TYPES AS PART OF A COMPREHENSIVE TRANSPORTATION
NETWORK.

ACTION 4.1.1
ENSURE FUTURE STREET CONNECTIONS
NEEDED FOR WELL-PLANNED
CONNECTIVITY IN DEL CITY ARE SECURED
OR MAINTAINED.
Ensure that when development occurs in Special
Focus Area 2 that Leonhardt Drive is extended from
the existing Hartsdale Neighborhood to 29th Street.
The new extension should at a minimum continue
the same character as the existing street, meaning
the same right of way width and configuration of
two lanes with drainage in between. Though new
sidewalks and other amenities may be deemed
appropriate bu the City of Del City. Consider
extending 4th street from Vickie Drive to North
Sooner Road if development occurs in that area.
Consider realigning the entrance for Eagle Lake Park
with Scott Street to lead directly into the park.
ACTION 4.1.2
COMPLETE A DETAILED INVENTORY
OF THE EXISTING SIDEWALK NETWORK
THROUGHOUT THE CITY.
Compile and map a detailed inventory of all
existing sidewalk and crosswalk segments in the
City. The inventory should include the type of
construction materials, width, presence or absence
of a landscape buffer between back of curb and
sidewalk, the presence of impediments to travel
(e.g., utility poles, landscaping, etc.), compliance
with American with Disabilities Act (ADA)
accommodations and a condition assessment.
This information should be mapped in Geographic
Information System (GIS) mapping software
and should be used to facilitate the creation of
a prioritized improvement program or master
planning efforts. The City along with the school
district should also submit a grant proposal to the
Safe Routes to School (SRTS) program to secure
external funding support.

ACTION 4.1.3
PROACTIVELY PURSUE MUTUALLY
BENEFICIAL TRANSPORTATION PROJECTS
WITH AREA PARTNERS AND MUNICIPAL
NEIGHBORS.
Transportation corridors, travel patterns, and
mobility issues typically do not end at city limits
and often cross into multiple jurisdictions. This
is the case in Del City, where its arterial roads
connect with and continue into Oklahoma City
and Midwest City. Local transportation issues
are also often experienced by municipal and
area neighbors and may be best and most costeffectively addressed through coordination and
partnerships. Opportunities for partnership on
shared transportation projects could include road
maintenance, traffic counts, corridor improvements,
coordinated traffic signals, wayfinding signage,
sidewalk and trail network connections, and new
transit services.
ACTION 4.1.4
IMPLEMENT AND ENCOURAGE TRAFFIC
MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES THROUGHOUT
THE CITY TO IMPROVE SYSTEM FLOW AND
EFFICIENCIES.
Traffic management strategies could include
annually tracking transportation system
performance statistics (such as traffic counts,
average speeds, and crashes) to program
maintenance and design improvements, developing
a local intelligent transportation system (ITS) to
monitor transportation patterns and coordinate
traffic signals for efficient movements, and consider
implementing an access management plan to
strategically locate and limit driveways and access
points on arterials. 

ACTION 4.1.5
INCORPORATE BIKE RECOMMENDATIONS
INTO FUTURE UPDATES OF APPLICABLE
TRANSPORTATION PLANS.
Ensure bicycle and pedestrian facilities are
considered in future transportation planning
documents developed by Del City, the Oklahoma
Metropolitan Planning Organization, Association
of Central Oklahoma Governments, and Oklahoma
Department of Transportation.

section: STRATEGY 4.2: ENHANCE VISUAL AESTHETICS ALONG PRIMARY CORRIDORS TO CREATE ATTRACTIVE FRONTAGES.
text:
STRATEGY 4.2: ENHANCE VISUAL AESTHETICS ALONG PRIMARY
CORRIDORS TO CREATE ATTRACTIVE FRONTAGES.

ACTION 4.2.1
DESIGN AND IMPLEMENT ROADWAY
TREATMENTS TO SUPPORT PLACEMAKING
AND ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT ALONG
KEY CORRIDORS IN DEL CITY.
The visual impression of arterial streets are
especially important, because they play a role in
Del City’s public image. A community’s streets
are its assets and platforms for place making and
economic development, and strategic roadway
design treatments are critical to attracting and
ensuring the type of development desired by the
community at specific locations and along corridors.
Through implementing design guidelines specific to
the local functional classification system, consistent
designs can be implemented along major and
minor arterials, neighborhood collectors, and local
streets to best compliment the adjacent current and
future land uses. This will be particularly critical for
attracting and guiding development along arterials
with direct access to the turnpike and to ensure
the Olde Towne redevelopment has the necessary
treatments to foster placemaking and walkability.
ACTION 4.2.2
DEVELOP UNIFORM DESIGN STANDARDS,
DEPLOYMENT GUIDELINES, AND AN
INSTALLATION PLAN FOR STREET
FURNITURE AND AMENITIES TO
COMPLIMENT PEDESTRIAN AND BICYCLE
FACILITIES.
Street furniture and amenities—such as benches,
lighting, trash receptacles, bike racks, and
wayfinding signage—can greatly enhance the
pedestrian and cycling environment while providing
aesthetic elements to compliment streetscapes.
Whether at a district or along a corridor, amenities
and street furniture offer great tools to compliment
place-making efforts. To maximize the community
benefit of such an investment, it is important to
employ design standards and installation guidelines
to ensure the amenities present an attractive and
uniform image across the community and to locate
the amenities at locations where they will be actively
used by pedestrians and cyclists.

ACTION 4.2.3
CONSOLIDATE DRIVEWAYS, CURB CUTS,
AND PARKING LOTS TO CREATE A SAFER
AND MORE WELCOMING STREETSCAPE.
Large, underutilized parking lots represent space
for new, incremental outparcel developments that
reorganize major centers. Many corridors in Del City
have multiple driveways or curb cuts often close
to intersections. Reducing or eliminating parking
requirements and revising frontage standards are
policies that can support compatible redevelopment
on arterial commercial corridors. Consolidating curb
cuts allows for fewer turning conflict points, which
can increase safety and can even create more space
for landscaping, street furniture, and sidewalks.
ACTION 4.2.4
ENSURE THAT NEWLY BUILT STREETS OR
STREETS UNDERGOING CONSTRUCTION
OR IMPROVEMENTS ARE UPDATED TO
ACCOMMODATE MULTIPLE MODES OF
TRANSIT.
Commit to designing and constructing streets that
accommodate multiple modes—including vehicular,
pedestrian, bicycle, and transit—in the remaining
areas of new development and along corridors
being reconstructed during maintenance cycles or
with road improvement projects.

text:
GOAL 5: COMMUNITY IDENTITY
ESTABLISH A COMMUNITY IDENTITY THAT REFRESHES
DEL CITY’S IMAGE.

STRATEGY 5.1: REFRESH THE DEL CITY BRAND THROUGH INCREASED
EFFORTS OF PLACEMAKING AND QUALITY DESTINATION SPACES TO
ACHIEVE RECOGNITION AS A TOP TIER COMMUNITY.

ACTION 5.1.1
CONTINUE TO INVEST EFFORTS INTO
DEVELOPING A NEW COMMUNITY BRAND
FOR DEL CITY.
A well-crafted and authentic brand can help
Del City differentiate itself, attract investment,
foster community pride, and promote economic
development. Developing a community brand often
involves collaboration between local government,
businesses, community organizations, and
residents. To develop a successful community
brand, it is essential to involve key stakeholders,
conduct market research, and engage in a strategic
planning process. This process should capture the
essence and unique attributes of Del City, align with
community aspirations, and resonate with the target
audience. It should also consider the city’s values,
strengths, vision for the future, and the needs and
desires of residents and businesses.
ACTION 5.1.2
INVEST IN UPDATED GATEWAY
ENHANCEMENTS THROUGHOUT THE
COMMUNITY TO IDENTIFY DEL CITY TO
PASSERSBY AND ESTABLISH ENTRYWAYS.
Attractive gateway enhancements at key
intersections or entrances into town (i.e. exits from
Interstate 40) and special districts, such as the
future Olde Towne, and intuitive wayfinding signage
throughout the community provide opportunities
to uniquely brand and sell Del City as a great
community for visitors, residents, and businesses.
ACTION 5.1.3
PURSUE, PROMOTE, AND ENCOURAGE
PUBLIC ART AND DISTINCTIVE DESIGN IN
PUBLIC SPACES.
By partnering with schools or local artists to create
art for public spaces, such as murals, sculptures,
and interactive exhibits, the city can create
engaging and visually captivating environments for
residents and visitors to enjoy. 
ACTION 5.1.4
CONTINUE TO ENCOURAGE MORE
EVENT SPACES AND ACTIVITIES FOR
ENTERTAINMENT AND ATTRACTIONS
TO INCREASE LOCAL AND REGIONALLY
SERVING USERS.
Del City has numerous popular local events which
occur throughout the year such as the Trunk-orTreat or Tree Lighting Ceremony. Community events
like these help foster a sense of belonging, pride,
and social cohesion among residents. In addition to
supporting local events, the city should encourage
the development of family friendly event spaces and
activities. These events and activities draw visitors
from neighboring communities, increasing spending
in local businesses such as restaurants, shops, and
accommodations.
ACTION 5.1.5
RETAIN PROFESSIONAL SERVICES TO
DEVELOP REFINED GATEWAY STRUCTURE
DESIGNS FOR BOTH OLDE TOWNE AND
EAGLE LAKE TO HELP DEFINE A QUALITY
IMAGE FOR THE COMMUNITY.
These gateway structures serve as symbolic entry
points that welcome visitors and residents while
reflecting the unique character and identity of each
area. To develop refined gateway structure designs,
it is crucial to engage professional services, such
as architects, urban designers, and landscape
architects, who can bring expertise in creating
visually appealing and functional structures.
These professionals can work closely with local
stakeholders, including city officials, community
members, and business owners, to ensure that
the designs align with the community’s vision and
aspirations.

text:
GOAL 6: HOUSING & NEIGHBORHOODS
ENCOURAGE LONG-TERM NEIGHBORHOOD INTEGRITY WHILE PROMOTING A
WELCOMING COMMUNITY CHARACTER

STRATEGY 6.1: SUPPORT QUALITY NEIGHBORHOODS WHERE PEOPLE
LOVE TO LIVE THROUGH STRONG CHARACTER DESIGN, MARKETORIENTED AND FLEXIBLE ZONING POLICES, AND DIVERSIFIED
HOUSING TYPES FOR ALL AGES AND INCOMES.

ACTION 6.1.1
UPDATE ZONING REGULATIONS TO
INCLUDE NEW REGULATORY PROVISIONS
TO ALLOW A DIVERSITY OF HOUSING TYPES
(E.G., DUPLEXES, TRIPLEXES, TOWNHOUSES,
ACCESSORY DWELLING UNITS, ETC.)
TO DEVELOP TRUE LIFE CYCLE AND
WORKFORCE HOUSING OPPORTUNITIES.
Allowing a diversity of housing types through
flexible zoning promotes inclusivity and
accommodates the varying needs of residents
at different stages of life. By providing options
beyond single-family homes, Del City can offer
more affordable and flexible housing choices that
cater to individuals, families, and aging populations.
This promotes a sense of belonging and allows
residents to remain within the community as their
housing needs change over time. Flexible zoning
can be a catalyst for neighborhood revitalization
efforts. It enables the adaptive reuse of vacant
or underutilized buildings, encourages infill
development, and promotes the revitalization of
commercial corridors. By creating opportunities
for reinvestment and revitalization, flexible zoning
contributes to the revitalization and rejuvenation of
neighborhoods, enhancing their attractiveness and
economic viability.
ACTION 6.1.2
EVALUATE OPPORTUNITIES TO ENCOURAGE
TINKER AFB OFFICERS, FAMILIES, AND
OTHER SERVICE MEMBERS TO LIVE OFF-SITE
AND RETIRE IN DEL CITY.
The City should proactively evaluate opportunities
to entice Tinker personnel to Del City as part of their
initial transition to the area. Strategies could include
fostering a relationship with the base’s Housing
Services Office (HSO) and developing marketing
materials that highlights Del City’s diversified
housing options (including both rental and forpurchase) and quality of life amenities. Additional
strategies could include establishing commuter
transportation connections between Del City and
Tinker, developing partnerships to expand veteran’s
recruitment and placement services (as part of
an overall workforce development program), and
reoccurring special events and programming for
service members and veterans.

text:
STRATEGY 6.2: PROMOTE PRESERVATION AND REINVESTMENT IN
AGING NEIGHBORHOODS TO ENSURE NEIGHBORHOOD STABILITY,
LONGEVITY, AND PROTECTION OF DEL CITY’S HISTORIC FABRIC.

ACTION 6.2.1
CONTINUE AND EXPAND TRANSFORM
DEL CITY’S ADVOCACY-BASED CODE
ENFORCEMENT PROGRAM TO AID IN CODE
COMPLIANCE AND COMMUNITY CLEAN UP.
Many communities solely rely on an enforcement
approach to zoning code compliance (e.g., weed
abatement, debris removal, junk vehicles and
equipment, dilapidated housing, etc.). In these
communities, a code compliance officer identifies
a violation and issues a citation for compliance.
Reliance on this approach alone typically results in a
negative impression (and sometimes confrontation)
of what is otherwise an important governmental
responsibility. Del City’s existing efforts pairing
Code Enforcement to work with landowners to
achieve compliance when they cannot address the code issues on their own. The City should partner
with community members by providing resources
such as the neighborhood clean-ups, roll-off
dumpster assistance, tool sharing or rental, and
adopt-a-senior programs. These efforts are critical
to maintaining property values, pride in ownership,
and safety.
ACTION 6.2.2
CREATE AN ADVOCACY-BASED
NEIGHBORHOOD REVITALIZATION
PROGRAM WITH A TOOLBOX OF AVAILABLE
RESOURCES FOR AREA CITIZENS TO
MAINTAIN OR IMPROVE THEIR HOMES.
Similar to an advocacy-based code enforcement
program for individual home and business owners,
the City could offer a neighborhood revitalization
program containing a toolbox of resources for
homeowner associations and area residents. This
could include an infill/renovation program, housing
repair program, and accessibility assistance, and
substandard structure demolition, plus online
resources to help.
ACTION 6.2.3
CONTINUE TO MAINTAIN GOOD WORKING
RELATIONSHIPS WITH NEIGHBORHOODS
AND HOMEOWNER ASSOCIATIONS TO
FOSTER SAFE, VIBRANT, AND LIVABLE
NEIGHBORHOODS.
Establishing effective communication with area
neighborhoods and residents is an essential
component of government transparency and
operations. Communication is a two-way street. In
essence, the City needs to provide information to
the public, and should also be available and open to
listen to what the public needs and wants. As such,
the City should identify opportunities to ensure an
open dialogue with area neighborhoods and HOAs
so that they feel comfortable in expressing their
needs and to develop a cooperative relationship
moving forward. This could be an important
partnership opportunity to facilitate with the
neighborhood planning efforts. For example, while
there is a need for reinvestment and revitalization
in many areas of the City, west Del City has been an
area which has been in need of additional support.
Moving forward, the City should identify additional
opportunities to target public sector investment in
west Del City, and facilitate and encourage private
sector reinvestment and revitalization.
ACTION 6.2.4
CONDUCT NEIGHBORHOOD
IMPROVEMENT PLANS TO IDENTIFY ISSUES,
OPPORTUNITIES, AND STRATEGIES TO
ENHANCE DECLINING NEIGHBORHOODS.
Conducting neighborhood improvement plans is
a proactive approach to identify issues, leverage
opportunities, and develop strategies to enhance
declining neighborhoods. This process involves
engaging stakeholders, analyzing data, and
collaborating to create a comprehensive plan that
addresses the unique challenges and aspirations
of the community. Conduct a thorough assessment
of the neighborhood’s physical, social, and
economic conditions. Gather data on housing stock,
infrastructure, public spaces, amenities, crime
rates, economic indicators, and social services.
This assessment helps identify the strengths,
weaknesses, and areas for improvement within the
neighborhood.

text:
GOAL 7: PUBLIC SAFETY
PROVIDE EXCEPTIONAL POLICE AND FIRE PROTECTION SERVICES TO
ENHANCE PUBLIC SAFETY AND PROTECT THE DEL CITY COMMUNITY

STRATEGY 7.1: PROVIDE EFFECTIVE POLICE SERVICES TO PROTECT
THE HEALTH, SAFETY, AND WELFARE OF THE DEL CITY COMMUNITY.

ACTION 7.1.1
REGULARLY EVALUATE DEMAND ON POLICE
STAFFING TO ENSURE ADEQUATE STAFFING
LEVELS AND FACILITY CAPACITY TO
EFFECTIVELY RESPOND TO INCREASES IN
GROWTH AND ASSOCIATED CALL VOLUME.
As the police department grows, it will inevitably
need upgraded facilities and equipment to meet its
needs. In order to maintain its ability to efficiently
and effectively protect the community, Del City
should regularly conduct a formal evaluation of
police services to ensure adequate staffing levels
and sufficient facility capacity to perform the
necessary services. This would include evaluating
population growth of the City, GIS spatial analysis,
service calls, staffing levels, facility constraints,
and training needs. Assess the workload of
police officers, including the number and types
of incidents they handle, administrative tasks,
training requirements, and community engagement
responsibilities. Ensure that the workload remains
manageable and does not compromise the quality
of service. Assess the capacity of police department
facilities, including police stations, holding areas,
evidence storage, and training facilities. Consider
factors such as spatial limitations, technological
advancements, and community accessibility.
Determine if any upgrades or expansions are
necessary to accommodate increased demand. This
comprehensive analysis should be done annually
and can be included as part of the annual police
department report and summarized during the
budget request and evaluation process.
ACTION 7.1.2
CONTINUE TO MAINTAIN AND EXPAND
SUPPORT TO OFFER POLICE SAFETYRELATED COMMUNITY SERVICE AND
EDUCATIONAL PROGRAMS TO THE DEL
CITY COMMUNITY.
As is evident across the country and in Del City,
police departments do a lot more than just law
enforcement. The Del City police department
is involved with two annual National Night Out
events, Shop with a Cop, and is regularly involved
with the city’s six neighborhood associations.
Departments who are frequently involved in positive
outreach and engagement out in the community
are also the departments with the greatest citizen
relationships. In other words, it is these types of
events that build the most community support and
trust. Moving forward, the City should continue to
ensure there is adequate staffing and budget to
continue and expand these types of community
programs. These programs not only foster citizen
trust in departments and build positive relationships
but also serve as a recruitment tool for future
generations.

text:
STRATEGY 7.2: PROVIDE EFFECTIVE FIRE SERVICES TO PROTECT THE
HEALTH, SAFETY, AND WELFARE OF THE DEL CITY COMMUNITY. 

ACTION 7.2.1
REGULARLY EVALUATE DEMAND ON
FIRE DEPARTMENT STAFFING TO ENSURE
ADEQUATE STAFFING LEVELS AND FACILITY
CAPACITY TO EFFECTIVELY RESPOND TO
INCREASES IN GROWTH AND ASSOCIATED
CALL VOLUME.
In order to maintain its ability to efficiently and
effectively protect the Del City community, the
City should regularly conduct a formal evaluation
of services to ensure adequate staffing levels
and sufficient facility capacity to perform the
necessary services. This would include evaluating
population growth, service calls, staffing levels,
facility constraints, and training needs. Assess
the workload of the firefighters, including the
number and types of incidents they handle, training
requirements, administrative tasks, and any other
responsibilities. Ensure that the workload remains
manageable and does not compromise the quality
of service. Assess the capacity of fire department
facilities, including fire stations, equipment
storage, and training facilities. Consider factors
such as spatial limitations, maintenance needs,
and technological advancements. Determine
if any upgrades or expansions are necessary
to accommodate increased demand. This
comprehensive analysis should be done annually
and can be included as part of the annual fire
department report and summarized during the
budget request and evaluation process.
ACTION 7.2.2
CONTINUE TO MAINTAIN AND EXPAND
SUPPORT FOR COMMUNITY INVOLVEMENT
PROGRAMS FOR THE FIRE DEPARTMENT.
Similar to the need and extent for police department
community outreach and education, the Fire
Department is also involved in these types of
activities, including child car seat installation and
Fire Prevention Week. Moving forward, the City
should provide adequate staffing and resources
to ensure these types of community programs
are maintained or expanded over time. These
community service efforts and educational
programs promote positive community impacts
and increase citizen safety. Such efforts should be
prioritized and funded to maintain trust in these
institutions and provide education and support to
Del City citizens.

text:
GOAL 8: GOVERNANCE
ENSURE A FORWARD-THINKING, TRANSPARENT, AND ACCOUNTABLE CITY
GOVERNMENT

STRATEGY 8.1: MAINTAIN AN OPEN, RESPONSIVE, AND
ACCOUNTABLE MUNICIPAL GOVERNMENT ORGANIZATION
COMMITTED TO PERFORMANCE EXCELLENCE, BOLD VISION AND
INTENTIONAL DIRECTION.

ACTION 8.1.1
UNDERTAKE A COMPREHENSIVE UPDATE TO
THE ZONING REGULATIONS TO IMPLEMENT
THE COMMUNITY VISION IDENTIFIED
WITHIN THIS PLAN.
The comprehensive plan created a vision and
goals for what the built environment should be.
Six of the plan’s eight goals directly relate to land
development and would be supported by updated
zoning regulations. As such, it is critical that the
City evaluate and consider implementing the policy
and other regulatory recommendations as part of
a comprehensive update to the zoning regulations.
Sometimes, in the absence of a comprehensive
evaluation and update, there begins to be a
disconnect between the community vision for the
future and the regulations which help to implement it.
ACTION 8.1.2
CONSIDER CREATING A DEVELOPMENT
GUIDEBOOK WHICH CLEARLY SPECIFIES
THE DEVELOPMENT PROCESS FOR
LANDOWNERS, THE DEVELOPMENT
COMMUNITY, AND OTHER APPLICANTS.
For many people, particularly citizens, identifying
how to get something done on their property is
a confusing and overwhelming process. For the
development community, time is money. The quicker
and easier it is to navigate the process, the more
cost effective it is to spend more time and effort on
doing good things in the community. As such, Del
City should develop a comprehensive best practices
guidebook which clearly specifies the development
process per application type for both citizens and
the development community.
ACTION 8.1.3
REVIEW AND REVISE DEVELOPMENT
PROCESSES TO ENSURE PREDICTABILITY,
TRANSPARENCY AND EFFICIENCY.
A clear, predictable, and efficient development
review process is paramount to effective
citizen/developer interaction and satisfaction.
As previously mentioned, particularly for the
development community, time is money. Moving
forward, the City should continue to evaluate its
development review processes to ensure that
they are predictable, flexible (when appropriate),
transparent, and most importantly, efficient. This
could include a comprehensive evaluation of the
Planning & Development Department processes
and operations (i.e., a departmental workflow
organizational assessment) and may require
refinements to the zoning regulations or Code of
Ordinances. This could also include an evaluation
of opportunities to integrate better automation in
the review and approval process or other enhanced
technological innovations, where meaningful and
appropriate.

ACTION 8.1.4
ANNUALLY EVALUATE DEVELOPMENT
REVIEW FEES TO ENSURE ADEQUATE
RECOVERY OF COSTS AND LONG-TERM
FISCAL SUSTAINABILITY.
As a means to provide high quality services and to
make sure that Del City’s fees are commensurate
with the effort to process development
applications, the City should conduct an annual or
semi-annual fee update. The purpose is to ensure
adequate recovery of costs and long-term fiscal
sustainability.

ACTION 8.1.5
CONTINUE TO PARTNER, AND FIND
NEW PARTNERSHIPS, WITH LOCAL
AND REGIONAL ORGANIZATIONS TO
STRENGTHEN CONNECTIONS BETWEEN DEL
CITY AND THE SURROUNDING AREAS.
As mentioned in many previous strategies it is
always a good idea to maintain strong, open
relationships with neighboring entities. These
include but are not limited to Midwest City, Tinker
AFB, Oklahoma City, the MPO, and other regional
entities. Establishing effective communication with
local and regional organizations is an essential
component of government transparency and
operations and will help to change the negative
perceptions around Del City.

text:
STRATEGY 8.2: SUPPORT ADDITIONAL FOCUS AREA PLANS AND
SUBSEQUENT STUDIES TO IMPLEMENT THE COMPREHENSIVE PLAN
AND STRATEGIC PLAN GOALS.

ACTION 8.2.1
ENSURE FUNDING SOURCES AVAILABLE
TO IMPLEMENT THE NEIGHBORHOOD
PLANNING PROGRAM TO SUPPORT
ANY REVITALIZATION OF THESE CORE
NEIGHBORHOODS.
Identifying issues, potential projects, and other
solutions creates a solid blueprint for how to make
incremental improvements in these special focus
areas. However, the implementation of these
solutions will require extra effort in identifying
additional funds for capital investments. Beyond
the reliance on public funds only, seeking private
sector partnerships in the redevelopment process
increases the chance of real change being
implemented in a quicker timeframe.

ACTION 8.2.2
DEVELOP A PARKS, RECREATION, TRAILS,
AND OPEN SPACE MASTER PLAN.
As of this plan, Del City has not developed a
parks master plan, which is critical to prioritize
improvements and to be prepared when
funding opportunities arise. This plan should
comprehensively assess the existing parks,
recreation, trails, and open space system
and provide prioritized recommendations for
improvements and additional facilities based on
projected growth.
