text:
STRATEGY 8.3: CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROGRAMMING: ALIGN
DEL CITY’S CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM TO THE GOALS AND
FINDINGS OF THIS PLAN.

ACTION 8.3.1
ALIGN THE CIP TO IMPLEMENT THIS PLAN
AND OTHER MASTER PLANS THROUGH
CAPITAL FUNDING AND IMPROVEMENT
PROJECTS.
The Comprehensive Plan is intended to be a highlevel assessment of the entire city as a whole. It
is implemented through further studies (including
subarea plans), improved regulations, operational
changes and new policies, as well as through
capital investments. As the overarching guidance
document for the City, it does contain strategic
direction about future public sector investments
that will need to be implemented over time.
Some of these capital investments will be further
defined as part of a more refined sub-area plan
(e.g., A Trails Master Plan). However, other capital
investments will need to be derived from the
action recommendations identified in this Plan. As
part of an update to the CIP framework and the
development of the Implementation Work Program
staff will need to identify the projects identified in
this Plan to be included in the next update of the
Five-Year CIP.
ACTION 8.3.2
EVALUATE OPPORTUNITIES FOR
BROAD, CONSOLIDATED COMMUNITY
IMPROVEMENTS AS PART OF FUTURE
CAPITAL INVESTMENT PROJECTS.
Capital investments are often large scale, large
budget, and involve significant short-term
(sometimes long-term) impacts on overall quality of
life. Accordingly, the City should proactively identify
opportunities to consolidate multiple projects
(including quality of life investments such as
parks, trails, bike lanes, sidewalk replacement, and
improved lighting) into one overall investment. This
can result in improved operational efficiencies, cost
reductions, and minimized disruptions to the Temple
community. One way to ensure this is to establish
priority ranking criteria in the CIP which places
greater emphasis on capital investments which
solve multiple issues or utilizes multiple funding
opportunities.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
STRATEGY 8.4: UTILIZE THE ANNUAL BUDGET PROCESS AND
ESTIMATES TO STRATEGICALLY IDENTIFY AND PLAN FOR IMMEDIATE
AND MID-TERM OPERATIONAL NEEDS.

ACTION 8.4.1
PREPARE ANNUAL DEPARTMENTAL BUDGETS
AND FIVE-YEAR BUDGET PROJECTIONS
FOLLOWING DIRECTIVES FROM THIS PLAN
TO ENSURE ALIGNMENT AND COMPLIANCE
WITH LONG-RANGE PLANNING GOALS.
Similar to the benefits of planning weekly, rather
than just daily, it is critical that the City continue
to utilize its Annual Budget process to ensure a
fiscally-sustainable future of effective community
infrastructure and services. However, to ensure that
the community’s vision for the future (embodied
in this Plan) gets implemented over time, the
Annual Budget process should be combined
with the Annual Progress Report and Annual
Plan Amendment processes. This ensures that
departmental operations and their future proposed
work program dovetails with community priorities
for the future. In other words, an annual update
to the Implementation Action Plan should be
undertaken to prepare an updated list of priorities
to be initiated during the following year. This update
to the Implementation Action Plan should be
undertaken at the end of the calendar year so that
newly identified initiatives can be included in the
next annual budget request process.

ACTION 8.4.2
HIRE OR CONTRACT SERVICES FOR A
GRANT WRITER TO ASSIST THE CITY IN
FINDING AND APPLYING FOR GRANTS TO
FUND PLAN IMPLEMENTATION.
Numerous grants are accessible at both the
state and national levels, presenting a valuable
opportunity for the city to secure additional
funding. However, the process of identifying,
applying for, and managing these grants is
demanding and requires dedicated attention and
expertise. Due to the complexity of grant funding,
it is imperative for the city to consider hiring a
knowledgeable professional to streamline the
grant-seeking process. By bringing in an expert in
grant acquisition and administration, the city can
enhance its chances of successfully securing vital
funding and implementing impactful projects for the
community.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
STRATEGY 8.5: MAINTAIN COMMUNITY BUY-IN TO A LONG-RANGE
PLANNING STRATEGY THROUGH CONTINUING EDUCATION,
ANNUAL UPDATES, AND DECISION-MAKING CONSISTENT WITH THE
COMPREHENSIVE PLAN.

ACTION 8.5.1
CONSIDER AMENDMENTS TO THE
FUTURE LAND USE PLAN FOLLOWING
THE FRAMEWORK ESTABLISHED IN THE
IMPLEMENTATION CHAPTER OF THIS
PLAN, TO ALLOW FOR FLEXIBILITY AND
CHANGING CONDITIONS.
The Future Land Use Plan has a significant
impact on the future of the Del City community.
In association with the Thoroughfare Plan, the
Future Land Use Plan may be the most important
component of the entire Comprehensive Plan,
providing the framework to achieve the future
character of the City. When fully implemented
through updates to the zoning regulations and
code of ordinances (i.e., to achieve the identified
intent and character, land use types, and design
characteristics of each category), the Future Land
Use Plan becomes the primary guide for future
zoning decisions. When fully implemented through
code revisions that implement the desired character
framework, the Future Land Use Plan then signals to
the market to determine the development/building
types, design, and configuration to achieve it. The
process should follow the plan implementation
process in Chapter 5, Implementation Plan.
ACTION 8.5.2
TRACK COMPREHENSIVE PLAN
IMPLEMENTATION TIMING AND
PROGRESS OF INITIATIVES THROUGH A
COMPREHENSIVE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM.
Ensuring continual tracking and assessment
of progress at the staff level is critical to plan
success. The City of Del City is going to need clear
directive at the staff level to keep track of the
timing, progress, and coordination for each of the
initiatives listed in this plan. A software system (e.g.
Smartsheet) that manages the responsible lead and
involve departments, costs, alignment, and status
is a key component of this implementation plan.
This will make an annual progress report process a
streamlined and coordinated effort for the public.
ACTION 8.5.3
ESTABLISH AN ANNUAL PROGRESS
REPORT PROCESS TO BENCHMARK
COMPREHENSIVE PLAN IMPLEMENTATION
THROUGH INTEGRATION OF APPLICABLE
DEPARTMENTS, ACCOUNTABILITY, AND
COMPREHENSIVE PUBLIC REPORTS ON
PLAN PROGRESS AND AMENDMENTS.
The difference between a plan that gets
implemented and a plan that sits on the shelf
is accountability. Accountability requires a
defined process and responsible parties. As
such, the City should establish a defined annual
reporting and benchmark process to track plan
implementation over time. The process should
follow the plan implementation process in Chapter
5, Implementation.

ACTION 8.5.4
ESTABLISH AND UNDERTAKE A STAFF-LED
ANNUAL COMPREHENSIVE PLAN UPDATE
PROCESS.
After the Annual Progress Report is prepared, the
City should undertake an Annual Plan Amendment
of the Comprehensive Plan. At a minimum, this
should include an updated Implementation Work
Program, which identifies near- and mid-term
actions to be undertaken during the following fiscal
year or soon thereafter, in addition to any changes
necessary to the maps created in this Plan. This
process should be a deliberate, strategic, and public
process that continues to keep the comprehensive
plan at the forefront of City decisions. The process
should follow the plan implementation process in
Chapter 5, Implementation Plan.
ACTION 8.5.5
ESTABLISH AND UNDERTAKE A STAFF-LED
5-YEAR PLAN UPDATE PROCESS WITHIN FIVE
YEARS AFTER PLAN ADOPTION.
Beyond the Annual Plan Update, a broader
evaluation and update should be prepared every
five years. The 5-Year Plan Update is intended to
be a comprehensive review of the existing plan
to assess its successes and shortcomings with
regard to implementation of the vision, goals,
and commitments. The process of the 5-Year
Plan Update should follow the outline provided in
Chapter 5, Implementation Plan.
ACTION 8.5.6
UNDERTAKE A 10-YEAR PLAN UPDATE
WITHIN 10 YEARS AFTER PLAN ADOPTION.
Capturing, planning, and implementing the
community’s vision for the future is one of the most
important actions a City government can undertake.
In this regard, this Plan sets the stage for all
subsequent implementation actions during the 20-
year planning horizon. That being said, conditions,
population composition, and City trends and
concerns change over time. In order to ensure that
the Comprehensive Plan continues to provide the
best and most appropriate guidance possible, the
Plan should be taken through a full update process
every 10 years.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
## Goal 3: Placemaking

Del City is largely composed of commercial corridors and residential neighborhoods with most of the city completely built out. Cities with limited greenfields for growth and development tend to focus on redevelopment and revitalization strategies. Developed areas can evolve to suit the changing preferences and market context for Del City. This evolution often requires careful planning, community engagement, and public-private partnerships.

Stakeholders from Del City and the planning team explored how this evolution might take place by examining opportunities and priorities for three selected focus areas. Building on research and stakeholder interviews to develop the context of each focus area, participants convened for a one-day workshop to brainstorm about priorities. Workshop results were reviewed by a joint meeting of City Council and Planning Commission, and refined for delivery as concept plans in this chapter.

---

## Reno Avenue

The Reno Avenue focus area is a two-mile corridor of residential neighborhoods combined with light industrial uses and some commercial uses. Del City’s Reno Avenue spans from Oklahoma City, where it approaches the developing Oklahoma River, to Midwest City, where new commercial development is underway. It connects recreational amenities like Eagle Lake and Ray Trent Park. Development and population density ends north of Reno due to the presence of the floodplain, but some new development is feasible in the area. Participants discussed three primary strategies to advance Reno Avenue:

- Transform Eagle Lake into a recreation destination  
- Enhance the Reno Avenue streetscape to welcome and connect  
- Support strong neighborhood identity and strengthen commercial properties to shift away from industrial uses  

---

## 29th Street

The 29th Street focus area examines two separate locations on the corridor: the Olde Towne commercial district around Epperly Drive, and a large undeveloped area west of the Sunnylane Cemetery. Epperly Drive features a great building configuration and anchor tenants to support revitalization of a Main Street destination for Del City. The large undeveloped area is one of the few remaining large tracts in Del City. Participants discussed a few strategies to advance 29th Street redevelopment:

- Invest in creating a Main Street destination at Olde Towne  
- Leverage vacant property to add to Del City’s diversity of housing and neighborhoods  

---

## 15th Street

The 15th Street focus area is a 1-mile corridor of commercial uses, featuring civic buildings and amenities like the library, community center, and connections to Ray Trent Park. This segment of 15th Street has excellent access to Interstate 40, Tinker Air Force Base, and through a regional trail connection even to Lake Stanley Draper. Participants discussed a few strategies to advance 15th Street:

- Envision 15th Street as the heart of a visionary city park system

text:
CONTEXT
This 2-mile corridor of Reno Avenue spans from
Crooked Oak Creek in the west (Oklahoma City
border) to Sooner Road in the east (Midwest City
border). Key features include access to Interstate 40
via Scott Street and via Sunnylane, Eagle Lake Park,
residential neighborhoods, Ray Trent Park, and
Sooner Market shopping center at Sooner Road.
To the west, Reno passes over the Oklahoma River
and through an industrial area along Interstate 40
before entering Bricktown 2.5 miles from the focus
area. The First Americans Museum & upcoming
OKANA Resort are just 1.5 miles west of the site.
To the east, Reno passes through to Midwest City
neighborhoods and commercial intersections. The
following strategies and imagery represent only
a few potential ideas for the future of this area.
As time goes on, these areas could develop in a
multitude of ways. It is likely, the area around Reno
Avenue will retain some of its industrial charm but
as redevelopment occurs, this area could look very
different in the future.
CONNECTIONS
Reno Avenue conveys about 9,000 - 12,000 vehicles
per day in this area. The focus area has access
to Interstate 40 via Scott Street and the Tinker
Diagonal frontage road. Sunnylane Road carries
a similar traffic count south of Reno Avenue while
Sooner Road carries about 16,000 vehicles per
day south of Reno Avenue. Interstate 40 in this
area carries about 86,000 vehicles per day. A
combination of abandoned and active rail lines run
north of the study area. Part of these rail lines are
under consideration as a possible future corridor
for multi-purpose trails or commuter rail corridors.
It is important to note the rail line east of Sunnylane
Road and south of 4th Street is abandoned
and portions in Midwest City have already been
converted into trails.
COMMUNITY CONVERSATION
Through a community open house and stakeholder
interviews, participants discussed the following
topics related to development of the Reno Avenue
area.
Desires
• Restaurants, retail, and business development
along the corridor. Some participants also
mentioned hotels and art studios/galleries for
this area.
• Connect with Oklahoma City and Midwest City
bicycle trail systems.
• Sidewalks or bicycle connectivity along Reno.
• Build from the energy of ongoing Midwest City
development at Sooner & Reno.
• Concert venues, police substation, updated
aesthetics
Assets
• High accessibility to regional transportation.
• Eagle Lake park amenity.
• Views of downtown Oklahoma City and
Oklahoma River valley.
• Nice neighborhoods like Midway Village.
Challenges
• Low traffic and low residential density, especially
to the north, limits commercial activity.
• Low quality aesthetic impression along parts of
Reno Avenue.
Opportunities
• Connections to Oklahoma River & regional trails,
tied to public space enhancements at Ray Trent
Park.
• Improved gateways, streetscapes, and aesthetic
screening.
• Developing vacant properties for new uses,
potentially housing to increase residents/
customer base in Del City.
• Renovating legacy commercial properties.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
STRATEGY 3.1: TRANSFORM EAGLE LAKE INTO A DESTINATION

Eagle Lake Park is a scenic destination that features a peninsula shaped like an eagle’s head, which
coincidentally ties to the local school mascot the Eagles. The peninsula has a restroom facility,
fishing facilities, picnic areas, and playgrounds. The lake is stocked with fish, attracts birds and
wildlife, and has views of the downtown Oklahoma City skyline. A stretch of industrial, automotive,
and storage buildings line Reno Avenue in the vicinity of Eagle Lake. The area has some consistent
tenants, but experiences high turnover in the buildings which are a mix of metal industrial buildings
and brick structures. Several enhancements are proposed for further consideration, to transform
the park into a family-friendly destination.

ACTION 3.1.1
CONNECT TO THE REGIONAL TRAIL SYSTEM
Oklahoma City has an 80-mile trail network, offering connectivity to destinations like the First Americans
Museum and Scissortail Park. A trail connection at Eagle Lake will offer Del City residents access to
this network, and make Eagle Lake the front door of Del City for people enjoying the trail system. Install
trailheads with maps of Eagle Lake Park amenities demonstrating the connection to the regional trail
network.

ACTION 3.1.2
CONSTRUCT AN ATTRACTIVE AND VISIBLE ENTRANCE AT RENO AVENUE
Eagle Lake Park needs a visible, attractive entrance and approach from Reno Avenue to capture attention
and create a welcoming sense of place. Consider options for an eye-catching signature landmark, piece of
art, or sign that reflects Eagle Lake’s place identity. Add landscaping along the edges of the drive to screen
existing industrial buildings.

ACTION 3.1.3
ADD PARKING AND FOOD TRUCK
INFRASTRUCTURE
A new parking lot can also include electricity and
parking pads for a couple of food trucks, which
will allow for easy food service during events and
programs in the park. Pavilions and tables also
support food truck operations. In the long term, Del
City may consider adding a permanent dining or
concession.
ACTION 3.1.4
INSTALL NEW AMENITIES AND LANDSCAPE
FEATURES TO SUPPORT ACTIVATION OF
THE PARK
The most successful parks have infrastructure to
support frequent programming and activation by
the city or by community groups. Explore these
enhancements to facilities at Eagle Lake that can
encourage existing activities and invite some new
ones. These amenities support fishing, walking,
biking, watching fireworks or viewing the skyline
from afar, lounging and sitting, hosting community
nights, playing yard games, and more.
Consider the following amenities. Supporting
images are included here and on the next page.
• Sandy beach for visual connection to water.
• Landmark hill for views of skyline and active
play.
• Lawn game library and lawn game courts for
users to initiate activities anytime.
• Public art or architectural feature to create a
landmark photo opportunity and memorable
view.
• Dense landscaping and vegetation along the
north edge of the park to screen industrial uses.
• Permanent lighting for event-based nighttime
uses and security.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
STRATEGY 3.2: ENHANCE THE RENO AVENUE STREETSCAPE TO
WELCOME AND CONNECT
Reno Avenue is a common through-traffic route which connects Del City, Midwest City, Bricktown,
and other parts of Oklahoma City. Currently, the street is aesthetically uninviting and does not
serve the new image that Del City wants to represent. Reno Avenue also forms a direct connection
between recreational amenities like Eagle Lake and Ray Trent Park.
Given the connection to multiple neighboring communities and recreational assets for Del City,
new streetscape can create a more attractive and welcoming experience, connect people to local
assets, and greatly help to rebrand Del City’s image. Further study will need to be done to determine
the most appropriate reconfiguration. Factors will include but are not limited to traffic impact,
neighborhood desires, right-of-way constraints, and constraints from funding sources.

ACTION 3.2.1
RECONFIGURE RENO AVENUE TO SUPPORT
SAFE TRAVEL, CONNECTIVITY, AND
AESTHETIC APPEAL
Reno Avenue has the potential to become a
welcoming avenue into Del City. With the lowest
vehicle count per day of the three focus areas, there
is ample room for a road diet to add pedestrian and
cyclist infrastructure and maximize safety. Further
study will need to be done to determine the proper
configuration but the image below respresents
one possible option for minimizing turn conflicts,
increasing pedestrian and cyclist connections,
and creating an inviting streetscape for people to
connect to Eagle Lake and Ray Trent Parks.
The image below represents one potential option
to reconfigure Reno Avenue. Many configurations
could serve the city’s needs and further study
will need to be done. When deciding which
configurations would be the following criteria
should be used.
• Consider reducing the number of through-travel
lanes to one in each direction to prioritize safe
pedestrian and cyclist connections.
• Consider adding a landscaped median with
intermittent left turn lanes to allow traffic flow
and decrease turn conflicts.
• Add a multi-purpose trail, or sidewalk and
bicycle lanes. It is possible to use grants to
help fund these improvements. Be aware that
standards for safety will change depending on
the source of funding.
• Prioritize additional safe crosswalks, including
the possibility of a stop sign or signal at
entrances to Eagle Lake and Ray Trent Parks.
• Consider opportunities for enhanced gateway
signage, public art, and landscaping of
neighborhood entrances or commercial building
frontage.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
STRATEGY 3.3: SUPPORT STRONG NEIGHBORHOOD IDENTITY AND
STRENGTHEN COMMERCIAL PROPERTIES
Reno Avenue consists of many residential and commercial properties. In order to enhance
the neighborhood identity, implementing effective branding strategies and making aesthetic
enhancements would benefit the area. As Reno Avenue begins to change, supporting new land uses
can improve neighborhood identity and strengthen the community.

ACTION 3.3.1
SUPPORT AESTHETIC IMPROVEMENTS
TO PROPERTIES WITH RENO AVENUE
FRONTAGE
Consider a matching grant or other incentives that
encourage property owners to enhance building
frontages and facades with landscaping, paint, or
artwork.
ACTION 3.3.2
ENHANCE NEIGHBORHOOD BRANDING
Consider a grant to support landscaping, gateway
signage, or street sign toppers to enhance the
identity of neighborhoods along Reno Avenue.
Midway Village once had its own branding,
identity, and signage that made it distinct from its
surroundings.

ACTION 3.3.3
SUPPORT EVOLUTION OF LAND USES WITH
NEW HOUSING, SMALL-SCALE INDUSTRY,
AND RENOVATED COMMERCIAL BUILDINGS
Facilitate evolution of land uses for undeveloped,
commercial, and/or industrial properties in need of
repositioning.
Undeveloped properties in this area may be
candidate sites for multi-family housing or mixeduse. Presently, commercial activity along Reno
Avenue is hindered by a lack of residential density
to the north.
Industrial properties in this vicinity might be well
suited for breweries and makers who like buildings
with access to recreation and amenities.
Existing shopping centers are often extremely
adaptable, and have the bones to accommodate
new businesses with proper maintenance,
renovation, and marketing.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
CONTEXT
The 29th Street corridor spans the entire width of
Del City from Bryant Avenue to Sooner Road. To the
west, 29th Street passes toward Oklahoma City’s
Trosper Park and connectivity with Interstate 35
frontage roads. To the east, 29th Street connects
to Tinker Air Force Base entrances, Interstate 40,
and the heart of Midwest City’s “Original Mile”
commercial development. Key features of this area
include large employers like MTM Recognition, Del
City’s original commercial district once known as
Olde Towne, and a large undeveloped area on the
south side of SE 29th Street west of Sunnylane
Cemetery. The undeveloped site is of particular
interest, and currently consists of forested and
grass areas, a drainage canal, and an inactive
oil pump site. A portion of the site is located in a
floodway. This study area focuses on two of the
sites mentioned above: the original commercial
district around Epperly Drive and the large
undeveloped site. The following strategies and
imagery represent only a few potential ideas for the
future of this area. It is likely, the area around 29th
Street will retain some of its existing charm but as
redevelopment occurs, this area could look very
different in the future. The city is open to complete
and total renovation of this area should the right
opportunity arise.
CONNECTIONS
Traffic counts on 29th Street vary throughout Del
City. Traffic is lighter west of Sunnylane Road,
around 10,000 vehicles per day. East of Sunnylane
Road, traffic is higher, around 15,000 - 20,000
vehicles per day. It is important to note that 29th
Street heading east from Del City connects directly
to Midwest City and Tinker Air Force Base. The large
undeveloped site in this focus area is about two
miles from Interstate 40 connectivity, via Sunnylane
or Air Depot roads. It is also about two miles from
Interstate 35 connectivity, via on-ramps at 25th
Street or Grand.
COMMUNITY CONVERSATION
Through a community open house and stakeholder
interviews, participants discussed the following
topics related to development of the 29th Street
area:
Desires
• A new housing subdivision in the undeveloped
areas.
• Preserve the importance of MTM Recognition
and other anchor businesses.
• Enhance the area around Epperly Drive as
“Downtown Del City” or “Olde Towne” with
potential for streetscaping, town square, public
artwork, and new businesses that could attract
visitors.
Assets
• High commercial density between Sunnylane
and Sooner Road.
• “Downtown” area along Epperly Drive.
• Musical history with Castle Row Studio, Country
Western Hall of Fame.
• Vacant site for redevelopment.
• Hartsdel neighborhood & Sequoyah Park.
Challenges
• Restaurant options are often limited to small/
fast food options, but people increasingly want
full service restaurants.
• Buildings and public realm need updates.
• Homes are smaller than market prefers.
Opportunities
• Hometown feel for Epperly Drive with banners,
streetscaping, festivals, and events.
• Adding services and experiences for family fun
(e.g., Chicken & Pickle, etc.).
• Adding residential density in vacant areas.
• Better connections from some neighborhoods.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
STRATEGY 3.4: INVEST IN A MAIN STREET DESTINATION AT OLDE
TOWNE
The environment around Epperly Drive is the oldest commercial district in Del City, and it is built
similarly to a traditional main street district. Local businesses and community events already
establish these blocks as special destinations for residents. Strategic investments can emphasize
and accelerate development of this area into a new Main Street destination for Del City.
ACTION 3.4.1
DEVELOP A PLACEMAKING STRATEGY WITH FURTHER RESEARCH AND COMMUNITY
ENGAGEMENT
Olde Towne is worthy of special attention to develop a complete placemaking strategy. A placemaking
strategy should be developed in consultation with city staff, business owners, property owners, and area
residents. The strategy would include physical improvements alongside a consideration for the types
of programming and marketing that would advance the area’s development. The illustration below and
following pages represent options for consideration and further refinement through a community engaged
placemaking strategy.

ACTION 3.4.2
EMPHASIZE EPPERLY DRIVE AS A CLASSIC,
WALKABLE MAIN STREET ENVIRONMENT
Epperly Drive’s streetscape should accommodate a
clear sidewalk, pedestrian-oriented amenities, and
space for businesses to express themselves through
storefront elements or furnishings. Consider
reviewing city codes for any potential barriers to
businesses that want to enhance their storefronts
with outdoor merchandising, furniture, projecting
blade signs, window displays, or sandwich board
signs.

ACTION 3.4.3
CONSIDER A FESTIVAL STREETSCAPE
CONCEPT FOR EPPERLY DRIVE
Another option for Epperly Drive is a curbless,
festival street. A festival streetscape is a flexible
environment that can accommodate slow-moving
vehicles during most times, but always prioritizes
pedestrian and business activity. During special
events, festival streets easily convert to pedestrianonly environments with features like curbless edges
and removable bollards.
ACTION 3.4.4
REFRESH ROSE LANE AS AN ORGANIZED
AND ATTRACTIVE FRONTAGE ALONG 29TH
STREET
Rose Lane is a name given to a slip lane of parking
along busy 29th Street. Consider removing the
underused strip of slip lane parking and replace
with landscaped greenspace. Mid-century facades
include several great local businesses as well
as vacant space. The Rose Lane frontage area
represents an opportunity to rebrand the gateway
to Del City’s Olde Towne historic commercial
district.

ACTION 3.4.5
EXPLORE A MULTI-PURPOSE “TOWN
SQUARE” PUBLIC SPACE AT THE TRIANGLE
NEAR THE FIRE DEPARTMENT AS A PLACE
FOR COMMUNITY PROGRAMS
The triangle-shaped lawn near the fire department
may be a great location for a town square that could
serve many functions. Consider the possibility of
this space as a multi-purpose public space that can
accommodate main street programs and events.
This area may also include a visual landmark.
ACTION 3.4.6
PURSUE A DISTRICT MANAGEMENT
APPROACH
Thriving historic districts rarely emerge without
a comprehensive management and operations
approach. Often, this includes a non-profit entity
or city staff member with time to dedicate to
organizing business and property owners to
achieve district goals. One approach is the FourPoint Approach advanced by the National Main
Street Center and Oklahoma Main Street program.
The Four Points include economic vitality, design,
promotion, and organization. These points guide
initiatives like development incentives, placemaking
improvements, branding and marketing, events, and
partnerships.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
STRATEGY 3.5: LEVERAGE VACANT PROPERTY TO INCREASE DEL CITY’S
DIVERSITY OF HOUSING AND NEIGHBORHOODS
With few vacant parcels large enough for residential development left in Del City, the city should
ensure these parcels are developed into land uses that maximize community benefits. These will be
context dependent but likely involve adding- new types of housing different from the existing stock
and mixing commercial into the neighborhood to increase sales tax revenue. The vacant parcels on
29th Street represent a perfect opportunity to achieve this.
There are some challenges on the site, like a floodplain, adjacencies to a cemetery, and nearby
industrial areas. However, it has the potential to tie into the Hartsdel neighborhood and has
attractive nearby features like Lassiter Drug, grocery stores, schools, Tinker Air Force Base access,
and highway access.
Due to market conditions and site challenges, a public-private partnership may be considered for
this property. Any public-private partnership should reflect Del City’s goals and local preferences for
high quality development that adds new options for current and future residents.

ACTION 3.5.1
PROMOTE A MIX OF DENSITY, USES, AND HOUSING STYLES ON THE VACANT PARCELS ON
29TH STREET.
Much of this site is currently zoned for large lot residential, but it is large enough to accommodate zones
of varying uses and densities. Larger developments can use this mix of product types to enhance their
economic performance and target several market segments across a project.
The market for small size, single-family homes in Del City has many options. Larger floorplans of greater
than 1,200 square feet are underrepresented in Del City. These larger floorplans can be accommodated in a
townhouse or apartment style near 29th Street, or in a single-family configuration adjacent to the Hartsdel
neighborhood. Areas closest to 29th Street are also suitable for a mixed-use configuration with limited retail
or office space as part of the product.
ACTION 3.5.2
ADDRESS STORMWATER ISSUES ON THE SITE WITH PLANNED PUBLIC GREENSPACES.
Across large sites, stormwater management strategies can play a double role as placemaking and public
space features. For example, Leonhardt Drive can be extended as a boulevard, from Hartsdel through the
floodplain, creating a signature public space that defines the new neighborhood.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
CONTEXT
The 15th Street Focus Area highlights a section of
15th Street that is slightly over one mile, stretching
from Sunnylane Road to Sooner Road and the
border with Midwest City. To the west, there are
residential neighborhoods of Del City and industrial
areas of Oklahoma City with connectivity to I-35
just 2.5 miles west of Sunnylane Road. To the east,
the City of Midwest City has incentivized successful
redevelopment with big box stores, a movie theater,
and restaurant parcels. The corridor features both
under-performing commercial properties with
potential for new tenants, and Del City’s most
important sales tax generator at Walmart. The
corridor also includes important civic spaces,
including the community center, the new public
library, a popular baseball tournament facility, the
Del City High School, and Ray Trent Park.
After discussions with stakeholders, residents,
and some additional research into the surrounding
area an opportunity for massive regional trail
connections were discovered. By adding just over
three miles of trail and/or sidewalk connections
Del City could create a strong regional linkage to
Oklahoma City, Tinker Air Force Base, and Lake
Stanley Draper.
CONNECTIONS
East of Interstate 40, traffic on 15th Street ranges
from 16,000 to 18,000 vehicles per day. West of
Interstate 40, traffic counts are lower at around
11,000 vehicles per day.
Ray Trent Park has existing bike and pedestrian
connectivity underneath Interstate 40 and to the
north with potential to connect to Eagle Lake and
Oklahoma City. Additionally, existing sidewalks and
trails connect the study area with Tinker Air Force
Base and Lake Stanley Draper.
COMMUNITY CONVERSATION
Through a community open house and stakeholder
interviews, participants discussed the following
topics related to development of the 15th Street
area:
Desires
• New restaurants and dining and new shopping
and retail.
• Emphasis on preserving and renovating midcentury historic assets, including “kitschy”
1960s buildings, commercial signage, and
interesting single-family homes.
• Connect high school, library, community center,
pool, soccer fields, and parks.
Assets
• Renovated and new commercial buildings, and
high commercial energy at Sooner Road.
• Del City Community Center and Patriot Park.
• Legacy mid-century signs and architecture.
• Population growth and diversity.
Challenges
• Buildings and public realm need updates.
• ODOT impact on this corridor due to Interstate
40.
• Troublesome land uses like storage facilities and
high quantity of marijuana dispensaries.
Opportunities
• Better signage for library entrance.
• Introducing artwork for bridges.
• Regional bike and pedestrian connections.
• Community events and festivals.
• Modern housing and commercial developments.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
STRATEGY 3.6: ENVISION 15TH AS THE HEART OF A VISIONARY CITY
PARK SYSTEM
Numerous civic assets in Del City are positioned near Ray Trent Park. By adding just over three
miles of trails and/or sidewalk and bike lanes, the city could invest in enhancing and connecting
these civic assets and neighborhoods to a regional trail network. 

ACTION 3.6.1
IMPLEMENT A WAYFINDING AND BRANDING
STRATEGY.
New branded wayfinding and markers would help
users identify and navigate to parks and public
spaces. Wayfinding signs and markers could further
emphasize the connections between civic spaces in
Del City and greater regional trails.
ACTION 3.6.2
EXPAND FACILITIES FOR MODERN
RECREATION PREFERENCES.
Northern portions of Ray Trent Park may represent
opportunities for new recreational features or
activities. A detention area on Crutcho Creek east
of Vickie Drive could present an opportunity to
install a managed wetland environment or other
uses compatible with periodic flooding.
Del City may consider acquiring or supporting
improvements to the privately-owned ballpark
facility, and reimagining it in light of floodplain and
parking access issues. Del City’s operations of
recreation facilities should maximize benefits to the
local community, present a welcoming and quality
image to visitors, and minimize disruptions for the
nearest residents.

ACTION 3.6.3
CONNECT TO THE OKLAHOMA
REGIONAL TRAIL NETWORK BY ADDING
CONNECTIONS BETWEEN THE OKLAHOMA
RIVER, EAGLE LAKE, RAY TRENT PARK,
TINKER AFB, AND LAKE STANLEY DRAPER.
The civic center of Del City is located on 15th
Street. In one small area there are many facilities of
importance to the local community. These include
Ray Trent Park, the pool and community center, the
new library, the high school, the ballparks, and local
shopping destinations. The Reno Avenue focus area
already highlighted potential connections between
Ray Trent Park, Eagle Lake Park, and the regional
trails along the Oklahoma River. These connections
can go even further. Just south of the special focus
area on 15th Street there are existing 12-foot wide
sidewalks which connect directly to Tinker Air Force
Base. And south of that are existing sidewalks
which stretch all the way to Lake Stanley Draper.
Only an additional 3.3 miles of sidewalks, trails, or
bike lanes are needed within Del City to create a
complete link which connects downtown Oklahoma
City all the way to Lake Stanley Draper. Not only
would this provide safe routes for children to
schools, parks, and the library but this trail system
would be an enormous tourism draw for Del City.
The following actions could be taken to pursue
regional trail connectivity.
• Add approximately two miles of trails and/or
sidewalks and bike lanes from Ray Trent Park to
Eagle Lake along Reno Avenue.
• Add new connections and access points from
neighborhood streets into Ray Trent Park.
• Add new creek crossings as needed.
• Improve the trail underpass below Interstate 40
with artwork or lighting.
• Enhance the crossing of 15th Street to extend
the trail to the ballpark and high school.
• Add approximately 1.3 miles of trails extending
from the southern edge of Ray Trent Park,
through the baseball fields and detention pond,
to Tinker Air Force Base.
ACTION 3.6.4
DEVELOP A MASTER PLAN FOR PARK AND
TRAIL IMPROVEMENTS TO FACILITATE
REGIONAL CONNECTIVITY.
Del City’s neighborhood parks are a great asset
to residential stability and attractiveness. A
comprehensive assessment of these facilities
would allow Del City to prioritize and schedule
improvements to parks and trails citywide. The
15th Street Focus Area demonstrates the potential
impact of such a study beyond improving parks,
because of the concentration of civic amenities
along 15th Street and west of Vickie Drive. These
include the community center, library, high school,
ballparks, and local shopping destinations.

section: QUICK WINS & BIG MOVES & ALIGNMENT WITH PLAN GOALS
text:
## Quick Wins & Big Moves

Redevelopment and revitalization of Del City focus areas will require careful planning, continued community engagement, and participation of both public and private partners. These strategies cannot be implemented overnight and most will occur incrementally over the course of several years. Many necessary changes depend on the initiative and financial commitment of private property owners and businesses. However, it often falls to the public sector to craft the strategy and make the first investments, leading the way to executing the vision.

Implementation of focus area improvements can unfold as a series of “Quick Wins” and “Big Moves.” Quick Wins might be small, inexpensive, or even temporary investments, like installing a hammock park at Eagle Lake Park. Big Moves are larger, more complex projects that might take bigger budgets and more planning to achieve, like a new streetscape for Rose Lane & Epperly Drive or a master plan for connected regional trails.

---

## Alignment with Plan Goals

The recommendations in these focus area concept plans align with the Envision Del City Goals discussed in Chapter 3, *Del City Reimagined*.

---

### Reno Avenue

- Transform Eagle Lake into a destination  
- Enhance the Reno Avenue streetscape to welcome and connect  
- Support neighborhood identity and strengthen commercial properties  

---

### 29th Street

- Invest in a destination on Main Street at Olde Towne  
- Leverage vacant property to add to Del City’s diversity of housing and neighborhoods  

---

### 15th Street

- Envision 15th as the heart of a visionary city park system

text:
## Plan Implementation

As Del City’s principal visioning document, the Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan will be referred to on a continuous basis to influence city policy, planning, regulatory and budgetary decision. Following the adoption of this plan, Del City’s elected and appointed officials and city staff will assume the primary roles of implementation and benchmarking. With these roles, there needs to be an understanding of the tasks associated with them, such as future review and decision-making, consideration of amendments and updates, and tracking progress to keep the plan current and relevant. This chapter of the plan contains a series of implementation actions and guidance for the City of Del City during the process.

---

## City Council

- Adopting amendments to the plan by ordinance, after the recommendation by the Director of Community Development  
- Adopting new or amended land development regulations to implement the plan  
- Approving inter-local agreements that implement the plan  
- Establishing the overall action priorities and time-frames by which each action item of the plan will be initiated and completed  
- Considering and approving the funding commitments that will be required  
- Offering final approval of projects and activities and the associated costs during the budget process, keeping in mind the need for consistency with the plan and its policies  
- Providing policy direction to the Planning Commission, other boards, and city staff  

---

## City Staff

- Managing the day-to-day implementation of the plan, including ongoing coordination necessary for plan implementation  
- Supporting and carrying out capital improvement planning efforts in accordance with the plan  
- Managing the drafting of new or amended land development regulations in accordance with the plan  
- Conducting studies and developing additional plans  
- Reviewing applications for consistency with the plan as required by the City’s land development regulations  

---

## Planning Commission

- Making recommendations related to development and redevelopment  
- Ensuring that development is consistent with the vision, goals, objectives, actions, and policies of this plan  
- Preparing a periodic progress report with the help of city staff to review goals to evaluate consistency with the plan’s vision

section: DEVELOPMENT PROCESS & SPECIAL AREA AND MASTER PLANS
text:
DEVELOPMENT PROCESS
There will likely be many occasions when this plan’s
directives require interpretation, adaptability,
and flexibility. These circumstances should be
thoughtfully approached and evaluated based
on compliance and implementation with the plan
vision, goals, commitments, and initiatives in
Chapter 5, Implementation Plan. Particularly
as it pertains to physical plans such as the
Future Land Use Plan, staff and decision-makers
in the development process will make daily
determinations interpreting the plan.
During the development process, proposals
will often be considered for developer and
landowner agreements, planned developments,
rezonings, etc. These are critical decision points
to ensure planning goals are achieved, the public
is informed, and processes are clear to promote
community investment from builders. City staff
should analyze applications for compliance with
the goals and commitments provided in Chapter
5, Implementation Plan. This process should
include evaluating an application by demonstrating
general compliance with the comprehensive plan
and reference to individual goals, strategies, or
even actions. 

SPECIAL AREA AND MASTER
PLANS
The Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan covers
a wide variety of topics which have the potential
to affect the city’s existing and future plans. This
includes additional plans and studies, some of
which are recommended in Chapter 3, Del City
Reimagined.
As implementation actions are initiated, the city
should ensure that actions within this plan consider
existing citywide service plans and special area
plans and work to align the two. Additionally, when
other city plans are being updated, those plans
should ensure that updates align with the goals and
actions defined in the Comprehensive Plan.

section: MONITORING AND BENCHMARKING & PLAN TO CODE RELATIONSHIP
text:
MONITORING AND
BENCHMARKING
The City of Del City will monitor the plan
implementation activities and the corresponding
effects on the community environment, community
prosperity, and community livability. Monitoring
activities will not only record implementation
accomplishments and measures of success but will
also reveal opportunities to modify and amend the
Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan to address
changing conditions or community preferences.
Realizing the vision of Del City involves actively
and deliberately working toward the action steps
in the implementation table. This plan created goal
statements and strategies – which culminated in
action steps. In the Implementation Action Table,
page 126, the departments involved in each action
are listed, providing guidance as to who shares
accountability in the outcome.
As part of the ongoing implementation process,
benchmarking and monitoring should take
place regularly and be summarized in a succinct
report. These reports should include a status
update on implementation, important actions
and accomplishments occurring in the past
year, and identification and recommendation
of needed actions and programs to be created
and implemented in the coming year. The
Comprehensive Plan report should be presented
at a work session and regular session of the City
Council.
PLAN TO CODE RELATIONSHIP
Though adoption of this plan is a big
accomplishment, the next step is to translate
this plan’s actions to the city’s development
code. While the Comprehensive Plan creates the
vision for community desires, the code enables
the city to enforce that vision. Once this Plan is
adopted, the city should immediately complete an
assessment of the current Development Code to
determine what is needed to meet the vision of the
new Comprehensive Plan. With this information
defined, the city can then move towards updating
the existing code to align with the vision of the
Comprehensive Plan. Additionally, as this plan is
updated over time, the code should be updated
accordingly, in order to reflect the changing
conditions in Del City.
As a part of the comprehensive planning process
an assessment of the city’s current zoning and
subdivision regulations was conducted. The
purpose of this assessment was to examine the
relationship of the existing zoning and subdivision
regulations to the policy directions of the
Comprehensive Plan, as well as the practices and
preferences of the community and its leadership. As
such, this assessment will narrow the gap between
the city’s policies and practices and seek to achieve
its objectives and preferred development and
procedural outcomes.
The following two pages features a high level
summary of the assessment and how it relates to
the comprehensive plan.

text:
GENERAL RECOMMENDATIONS
The following are general recommendations for the existing zoning and subdivision regulations. They do not
apply to a specific chapter.
1. Update Application Process and Procedures
Efficient and well-documented application processes
are integral to shaping the customer service culture
of a municipality, as they facilitate positive outcomes
for all parties while maintaining regulatory standards.
Additionally, since application processes and
procedures are updated periodically, it is important
that their documentation generally “live” outside of
the land development ordinance.
The following are strategies recommended to update,
streamline, and clarify application processes and
procedures:
• Create a development guide that summaries all
application policies and procedures.
• In the development guide, establish clear and
consistent review times for each application
types.
• Implement metric reporting to ensure that these
are being met internally.
• Move to accepting digital submittals only.
• Consider starting this by accepting via email and
the moving to an online permitting software.
• Analyze and update the current fee structure,
as needed, to better recoup the cost of staff’s
review time and effort.
2. Update Document Formatting and
Readability
The orderly formatting of any document is essential
to its consistency, readability, and user-friendliness.
Overall, it is recommended that the regulations
undergo a formatting overhaul, regardless of any
content updates.
The following are strategies recommended to update,
streamline, and clarify the document format and
readability:
• Use a heading hierarchy to distinguish sections
of information and to group like information
together.
• Create tables to consolidate information (e.g.,
land use chart, dimensional standards).
• Use graphics to convey information, where
appropriate (e.g., depicting setbacks, building
height).
• Use PDF bookmarks and cross-references in the
online document. These tools improve the user’s
ability to quickly navigate to desired sections and
information.
• Continue to maintain the Municode version or
other similar online program.
• Restructure the ordinances. Many of the chapters
and sections are out of optimal order based on
industry best practices.
• Update all definitions in the zoning and
subdivision ordinances to define all major terms
used in the document and to be consistent with
modern development and industry terms. It is
important to make sure that the definitions line up
with the city’s desired development types.
• The American Planning Association has a
Planner’s Dictionary. This publication is a good
starting point for developing definitions. It pulls
the best definitions of terms from around the
country. Definitions should be tailored to fit Del
City.
3. Restrictive Uses
Many of the permitted uses are far too narrow in
definition and restrictive, this is likely why Del City
is receiving a high volume of special use permits.
Reduce the number of special use permits required
by allowing some by right, others by conditional
allowance (with defined site or use restrictions), and
others remaining special use only where necessary.
Analyze the intent of the zoning district and
create a new set of uses categorized into allowed,
conditional, and special use. Use broader definitions
which fit industry standards. Create a table that
includes a list of all land uses by zoning district with
classifications for allowed, conditional, and special
use. Uses that are not listed in the land use table
would be prohibited. A list of prohibited uses can
be retained if the city desires, but it could be easily
interpreted that any use not listed as “prohibited”
is, be default, allowed in some manner and the city
would need to facilitate a location for such a use.

RECOMMENDATIONS FROM THE COMPREHENSIVE PLAN
The following are recommendations that are related to the recently adopted comprehensive plan. These
recommendations will help to implement the vision of the plan by affecting the built environment of Del City.
REVITALIZATION
1. Consistently utilize the Future Land Use Map
when reviewing development proposals and
rezoning applications to ensure development
is compatible with the city’s vision. Establish
language in the development ordinances that
rezonings or changes to land use are required to
comply with the comprehensive plan.
2. Update all references in the code from the
“Del City General Plan” to the “Del City
Comprehensive Plan.”
3. Ensure that zoning and subdivision regulations
are updated to support the vision established
in the Comprehensive Plan and the Future Land
Use Map. The Future Land Use Map places a
greater emphasis on character or form rather
than use, this allows for more flexibility in the
market and will diversify Del City’s tax base but
requires development codes to support this type
of development.
PLACEMAKING
1. Review the zoning regulations to ensure food
trucks and other similar uses are allowed in
parkland.
CONNECT
1. Examine the sign ordinance to strive for a
balanced approach that considers both the
aesthetic appeal of the city and the promotional
needs of businesses. Set clear guidelines
for sign type, size, height, and placement to
maintain visual harmony, consider special sign
regulations in Olde Towne. Define enforcement
mechanisms and penalties for non-compliance
to ensure that the regulations are effectively
followed.
2. Examine parking requirements in the
development code. Reducing or eliminating
parking requirements and revising frontage
standards are policies that can support
compatible redevelopment on arterial
commercial corridors. Consolidating curb cuts
allows for fewer turning conflict points, which
can increase safety and can even create more
space for landscaping, street furniture, and
sidewalks.
3. Ensure future street connections needed for
well-planned connectivity in Del City are secured
or maintained.
4. Ensure that newly built streets or streets
undergoing construction or improvements are
updated to accommodate multiple modes of
transit.
5. Develop uniform design standards, deployment
guidelines, and an installation plan for street
furniture and amenities to complement
pedestrian and bicycle facilities.
HOUSING AND
NEIGHBORHOODS
1. Update zoning regulations to allow a diversity
of housing types (e.g., duplexes, triplexes,
townhouses, accessory dwelling units, etc.)
to develop true life cycle and workforce
housing opportunities. Current regulations are
exclusionary and provide very little space for
housing types other than single family detached.
Consider moving towards more inclusive
or flexible zoning to allow the market to
determine housing typology and create greater
opportunities for density and redevelopment.
GOVERNANCE
1. Undertake a comprehensive update to the
zoning regulations to implement the community
vision identified within this plan.
2. Consider creating a development guidebook
which clearly specifies the development process
for landowners, the development community,
and other applicants.
3. Review and revise development processes
to ensure predictability, transparency and
efficiency.

text:
PLAN INTEGRATION
As this plan is put into action, an important
element of its success is the funding and annual
programming of implementation projects.
Components include annual budgets, capital
improvement program (CIP), departmental business
plans, and their ongoing alignment to achieve
effective outcomes.
ANNUAL BUDGETING
A primary source of funding for implementation
of this plan is the city’s annual budget, made up
of various revenue sources. For implementation
actions to be completed, the city must have
adequate funds to complete the necessary work.
It is recommended that each action taken by City
Council is tied to the goals of city’s comprehensive
plan, strategic plan and other relevant master
plans through direct language in resolutions and
ordinances.
CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM
A capital improvement program is a multi-year plan
(typically annual, 5-year and 10-year) that identifies
upcoming capital projects for prioritization and
funding. Projects include street infrastructure;
water, wastewater and drainage facilities; open
space, trails and recreation facility construction
and upgrades; and the purchase of major
equipment. Identifying and budgeting for major
capital improvements will be essential towards the
successful implementation of this plan. Decisions
regarding the prioritization of proposed capital
improvements should take into account the policy
and management directives of this plan to ensure
alignment between the two.

text:
This plan is only a snapshot in time. As the
community evolves, the plan must change as
needed to keep the community’s vision enact. In
order to remain consistent with the community’s
vision, implementation must be flexible and
allow for adjustments to account for shifts at the
local and national level over time. Therefore, it
is recommended that this plan be revisited on a
regular and routine basis followed by warranted
updates. 

FIVE-YEAR UPDATE
The purpose of the update is to evaluate the
original assumptions from the plan regarding
growth and development trends, emerging
issues, and to determine if remaining
proposed action strategies are still relevant.
The five year update should include the
following:
• A summary of plan amendments
and accomplishments to date;
• Changes in trends and issues since the
previous plan adoption (e.g., changes in the
predicted growth rate and the corporate
boundaries; demographic composition;
community attitudes; or other social,
environmental, or political issues which may
affect the feasibility of implementation);
• Conflicts between decisions made and
implementation actions yet to be undertaken;
• Changes in law, procedures, or programs which
will affect identified implementation actions; and
• A comprehensive re-evaluation of the
Implementation Action Plan.

TEN-YEAR UPDATE
The plan should be taken through a full
update process every 10 years. The 10-year
update should include the following:
• Comprehensive re-evaluation of the
community’s vision for the future;
• Evaluation of action items from this plan
that have yet to be considered;
• Re-engagement of the public;
• Re-analysis of the issues and trends related
to community growth, transportation, land
use, parks and recreation, public facilities
and services, and infrastructure; and
• A new plan of action reflective of the
refined vision for the future.

doc_title: Envision Del City Comprehensive Plan 2044 - reduced rev 2
text:
PLAN AMENDMENT
From time to time, it may be necessary for the
city to amend the overall plan in response to
changing circumstances, development trends, or
a unique opportunity. This will more than likely
occur in regards to the Future Land Use Map,
which is intended to be a durable implementation
tool yet not be too rigid that desired changes
cannot be accommodated. A development
process and amendment guide should be prepared
immediately following adoption for clarity regarding
amendments to the Future Land Use Map.
As time goes by, if there are only a few
modifications, the Future Land Use Map is still
serving its purpose. If there are significant
and frequent changes to the plan, a more
comprehensive evaluation should be undertaken
and the plan should be revised, with input from the
public.
AMENDMENT INITIATION
Amendments to the plan are typically requested by
a landowner, developer, or by the city itself. Most
commonly, these circumstances occur during the
development entitlement phase, where staff may
determine that a rezoning proposal is not consistent
with the comprehensive plan and the proposal
should either be altered for compliance or pursued
as an amendment. Determination of a necessary
amendment should be made by city staff when the
proposal is incompatible with the Future Land Use
Map. The process for such an amendment should
follow an established process formally approved
and codified by the city. The process should include
public hearings so that citizens have a chance to
voice their opinions. It is recommended that the city
maintain the threshold for a super-majority approval
for comprehensive plan amendments.
At times, the city may need to initiate the
comprehensive plan amendment process. Such
an action may be needed to amend the land use
framework, strategies or actions due to changing
circumstances, respond to state legislation
or mandates, etc. Such amendments shall be
consistent with the goals of this plan and citizen
input is a critical part of consideration of future
adjustments. Ongoing citizen involvement and buyin is critical to maintaining the support of the plan
as the city grows and evolves.

text:
IMPLEMENTATION ACTION PLAN
Successful implementation of the community’s
vision is the shared responsibility of the residents,
property owners, business owners, governmental
agencies, civic organizations, service providers,
trade groups, social advocates, and others. With
a 10-year implementation period for this plan,
however, it will take the leadership of the City of
Del City to remind its residents, stakeholders, and
partners that there is a role for all of them to pursue
the desired outcomes of their comprehensive
plan. This section of the plan contains the
Implementation Action Table. This table contains all
of the recommendations of the Plan prioritized and
assigned to the department that will be involved in
their completion.
Actions listed in the Implementation Action Table
are classified under the following broad categories:
• Program. These initiatives may include creating a new
program or expanding upon existing key city programs.
• Study. These initiatives may include conducting further
studies to identify solutions to particular problems.
• Policy. Official procedures or policies
used to make city decisions.
• Partnership. These initiatives will include
two or more entities working close together
to serve as champions of the plan.
• Regulation. Council approved regulations used
to direct growth or additional procedures.
The actions listed within the implementation tables
in this section are allocated among four levels of
prioritization and reflect the action’s priority as
determined during the comprehensive planning
process.
The three time frames identifies when
comprehensive plan actions should be initiated:
• Short Term. Actions should be initiated within
the next one to five years although completion
may extend across a larger time frame. These are
the city’s top priorities for implementation.
• Long Term. Most of these actions are projected to
be implemented in the next 5 to 10 or more years and
may be further prioritized in following plan updates.
• (Ongoing). Actions may also be ongoing where
they represent a policy or practice that should
occur throughout the planning period as a mark
in all of the above time frame categories.
